title: Design and Application of Network Topology Robustness Optimization Algorithms Based on Cheeger’s Inequality
authors: Huang Chaoqian, Wang Hanzhou, Dongyu Li
abstract: Cheeger’s inequality is used to estimate a lower bound on the number of edges that need to be cut off to split a regular graph into two subgraphs with the same number of nodes. Therefore, we design a network topology that is robust under malicious attacks based on Cheeger’s inequality. Considering that Cheeger’s inequality cannot be directly used to optimize complex networks in reality, we generalize the conclusion of Cheeger’s inequality and use this conclusion to propose the Good Regular Graph algorithm (GRG) for generating strongly robust network topologies under malicious attacks. In addition, we explain the design principle of the algorithm from a graph-theoretic perspective and give the corresponding network topology expansion algorithm. GRG, after appropriate processing, can be applied to optimize the substructures of a wide range of real-world network topologies to enhance their robustness. Experiments show that the algorithms proposed in this research perform better compared to other algorithms. We prove from both theoretical and experimental perspectives that Cheeger’s inequality can serve as a basis for designing robust network topologies under malicious attacks.

title: Construction of a Corrector for Distorted Situations in Micro-object Images Based on Transformation and Filtering Operators
authors: Rustam A. Safarov, Baxrom J. Mamanazarov, Bekzod I. Islomov
abstract: The scientific and methodological foundations were studied and methods, models, and algorithms for optimal identification of micro-objects were developed using Gaussian transformation and filtering operators, median filtering, fast Fourier transform, continuous and discrete wavelet transforms, and shift transforms. Mechanisms have been developed for using redundant information and structural components of an image based on the use of statistical and dynamic characteristics, which have advantages in reducing the complexity and labor intensity of information processing, identifying the redundant structural component, segmenting the image contour, growth dynamics, internal features and properties, visual delineation of approximations, and interpretation of objects. Computational schemes have been implemented that perform the functions of aligning histological sections, finding the contour of random situations and constituent objects, determining a set of levels and thresholds, performing segmentation, and conducting registration. Functional modules have been designed for graph search, image approximation based on wavelets, shear and other transformations, parameter determination, color coding and visualization of micro-objects. Algorithms for identifying, recognizing, and classifying microobjects were tested using real-world examples, specifically images of single-cell inflammatory cells (fibroblasts, fibrocytes) associated with lung disease. Signs of chronic inflammation, such as the presence of giant cells, were assessed. A software package for visualization, recognition, and classification of images of micro-objects has been developed and implemented. Its effectiveness has been studied under conditions of a priori insufficiency, parametric uncertainty, and low reliability of information.

title: Intelligent Analysis of Urban Green Zones Based on Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Data
authors: Ravshan Shirinboyev, Shavkat Akhralov, Asror Ulashev
abstract: Urban green zones play a critical role in maintaining ecological stability, improving air quality, mitigating the urban heat island effect, and supporting public health in rapidly urbanizing environments. However, continuous and accurate monitoring of urban vegetation remains a significant challenge due to heterogeneous land cover, shadow effects, and complex background structures typical of urban landscapes. Traditional ground-based surveys are time-consuming, labor-intensive, and often lack spatial completeness. This study proposes an integrated intelligent approach for the assessment of urban green zones based on unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) data. The proposed method combines spectral vegetation analysis using the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) with complementary color and texture features extracted from high-resolution RGB and multispectral imagery. Image preprocessing, vegetation segmentation, feature extraction, and classification stages are systematically integrated into a unified processing pipeline. Experimental results obtained under real urban conditions demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves vegetation health classification accuracy compared to conventional NDVI-based assessment. The integration of spectral, color, and texture descriptors enhances robustness against urban-specific distortions such as shadows and asphalt surfaces. The developed approach provides a computationally efficient, interpretable, and scalable solution for intelligent ecological monitoring and sustainable urban environmental management.

title: Method of Structural-Functional Design of Intelligent Industrial IoT Systems
authors: S. V. Kukartseva, A. V. Marchenko, T. I. Mariupolskiy
abstract: The rapid deployment of Industrial Internet of Things technologies in manufacturing enterprises requires systematic approaches to the design of distributed intelligent architectures. Existing solutions primarily focus on technological implementation aspects, while formalized methods for structural and functional synthesis of intelligent systems remain insufficiently developed. This paper proposes a structural-functional design method for intelligent Industrial Internet of Things systems operating in distributed industrial environments. The method is based on staged decomposition of the industrial object, structured allocation of data processing tasks, and rational distribution of computational resources between Edge and Cloud levels. A layered architectural model integrating sensing devices, communication infrastructure, distributed computational nodes, machine learning modules, and application-level services is developed. The proposed approach ensures systematic integration of intelligent analytics at the architectural design stage and supports scalability, modularity, and adaptability of industrial systems. The presented method formalizes design decisions under latency and resource constraints and provides criteria for functional allocation across distributed levels. The proposed framework can serve as a methodological basis for the development of intelligent, resource-aware, and scalable Industrial Internet of Things systems in smart manufacturing environments.

title: Experience in Synthesizing Combinational Self-Timed Circuits and Verifying the Results
authors: Leonid Plekhanov, Nikolai Morozov, Georgi Orlov
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of ensuring correctness in the self-timed (ST) digital circuit design, which provide a fault-tolerant alternative to conventional synchronous circuitry. The ST circuit's distinctive properties include an absence of logic hazards, sustained operability under extreme environmental conditions, and inherent stuck-at fault detection. These properties are achieved through a specific computational organization: dual-rail (i.e., two-phase) signal encoding, strict alternation between data and spacer phases, and explicit indication of the completion of transient processes. However, these properties render the ST circuit design and verification a nontrivial task. The paper observes a working method for automated synthesis of combinational ST circuits in synthesizable Verilog and proposes a comprehensive methodology for automated verification of the synthesized results. The verification approach is centered on two key aspects: preservation of logical functionality with respect to the original non-self-timed specification and satisfaction of internal self-timed correctness criteria. The methodology comprises the following stages: test generation for both the reference synchronous circuit and synthesized ST circuit, an ST protocol functional validation, evaluation of functional coverage achieved by the test suite, and automated regression analysis of circuit testing based on structured logging. The proposed approach aligns with modern coverage-driven verification practices. The methodology enables scalable verification across hundreds of test cases—a capability that is critically important during the active development of an ST circuit synthesizer.

title: Design and characterization of a portable fluorescence measuring system for the on-site monitoring of marine bio-constructors
authors: Davide Cassanelli, Paolo Rossi, Elena Cenni, Stefano Cattini, Sara Righi, Roberto Simonini, Alessandro Capra, Luigi Rovati, Cristina Castagnetti
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Marine bio-constructors are put at risk by natural disturbances caused by climate change and anthropogenic stressors. Given their fundamental importance in marine ecosystems, it is essential to reverse this trend. In this regard, measurement techniques capable of early detecting alterations of marine colonies are essential. Traditional underwater surveys rely on in-situ observations performed by divers, and in the last decade, such analysis has been increasingly supported by photogrammetry. Despite the significant benefits brought by photogrammetry and the notable progress of computer vision, to date, no measurement techniques fully succeeded in the early detection of minor alterations in the state of health. In this regard, fluorescence imaging has the potential to support and improve both ecological and physiological assessments. Nevertheless, to date, its use in the underwater environment is mainly exploited for qualitative nature photography and much less for quantitative analysis aimed at extracting biological information. One factor limiting the widespread use of quantitative fluorescence imaging to monitor marine bioconstructors is that studies in the literature generally do not allow for metrological traceability. Fluorescence intensity is usually reported in arbitrary units derived from the intensity of the acquired images. This study aims to support the transition from qualitative imagery to quantitative fluorescence imaging. To this end, we proposed introducing a simple and effective traceability obtained by relating the fluorescence intensity recorded by the instrument to the sodium fluorescein concentration that would generate the same intensity, namely the equivalent fluorescein concentration. This makes measurement results independent of the specific measuring system, enabling research conducted by different groups to be synergised.

title: Low-Complexity Channel Estimation for OTFS via Coordinate Descent Exploiting Delay-Domain Sparsity
authors: Masahiro Maruyama, Takanori Hara, Kenichi Higuchi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) modulation has attracted considerable attention for high-mobility wireless communications; however, accurate channel estimation (CE) remains challenging in the presence of fractional Doppler shifts. In the conventional embedded pilot-aided CE method, a low-complexity CE is achieved by a simple thresholding process, while its estimation accuracy significantly degrades due to inter-Doppler interference (IDI) induced by fractional Doppler shifts. In contrast, the conventional sparse Bayesian learning (SBL)-based CE method can effectively alleviate the performance degradation caused by the IDI by exploiting channel sparsity in the delay-Doppler domain. Nevertheless, the SBL-based method suffers from prohibitively high computational complexity, especially in wideband systems with a large number of subcarriers, which results in high delay resolution and a large estimation problem. To address these issues, we propose two efficient CE methods based on coordinate descent (CD), exploiting the inherent sparsity of OTFS channels in the delay-domain. The proposed methods reduce the dimensions of both the received signal and the measurement matrix through delay-domain thresholding. The first method, referred to as Reduced-CD, employs a fixed threshold designed to minimize the normalized mean square error (NMSE). The second method, referred to as Adaptive-CD, adopts two-dimensional (2D) CE framework and introduces an iterative thresholding process. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed CD-based methods significantly outperform the conventional threshold-based method in terms of NMSE and bit error rate (BER). Furthermore, they achieve significantly lower computational complexity than the SBL-based CE method while improving NMSE performance.

title: Pseudo-Chebyshev Functions over Finite Fields
authors: Juliano B. Lima, Daniel Panario, José R. De Oliveira Neto
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Circuits and Systems
abstract: In this paper, we introduce the notion of pseudo-Chebyshev functions over finite fields. In brief, such functions correspond to a generalization of the n-th Chebyshev polynomial, where n is not restricted to integer values, but can take on any rational value. Our approach is mainly based on concepts of trigonometry over finite fields. Besides defining the referred functions, we derive several of their properties and establish necessary and sufficient conditions for them to permute the elements of Fq. Potential applications of the proposed functions are preliminarily discussed.

title: Model-free Magnetic Servoing for Pose Control of Capsule Robots
authors: Chang Liu, Xiaoyang Wu, Jiaole Wang, Shuang Song
abstract: This paper proposes a model-free magnetic servoing technique for closed-loop pose control of capsule robots. The system employs an internal permanent magnet (IPM) inside the capsule and an external permanent magnet (EPM) manipulated by a robotic arm. Control feedback is directly derived from magnetic field measurements (via a sensor array) and optical tracking of the EPM's pose. To enable model-free control, we develop a feature extraction method that condenses the IPM's magnetic sensor data into a compact representation. Based on this magnetic feature, we derive a Jacobian matrix that directly maps the feature space to the robotic arm's joint configuration space. To adaptively handle system nonlinearities, the Jacobian matrix is iteratively updated using an unscented Kalman filter, bypassing the need for an explicit dynamic model. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, with average tracking errors of 0.62 mm in position and 0.75<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> in orientation during simultaneous pose control tasks.

title: Real-Time Hand Pose Estimation Using FMCW Radar on Resource-Limited Edge Devices
authors: The Tuan Trinh, Vu Le, Tien Dat Le, Xuanque Nguyen, Minhhuy Le
abstract: Real-time hand pose estimation is essential for natural human-computer interaction (HCI), especially in situations where vision-based systems face challenges due to lighting, occlusion, or privacy issues. This paper introduces RadarPose, an innovative framework that estimates 2D hand keypoints for HCI applications using raw Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar signals. To address the difficulty of manually annotating FMCW radar data, a cross-modal supervision strategy is implemented, utilizing synchronized RGB video frames processed by a pre-trained vision model to generate hand keypoint labels for training the radar signals. RadarNet, the core of this framework, is a lightweight convolutional neural network (L-CNN) designed to predict 21 hand keypoints, handedness, and hand presence directly from a raw data cube. By removing the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) stages, this framework facilitates end-to-end learning in the radar complex data domain, resulting in significantly reduced latency. Once trained, the proposed framework relies solely on radar data for inference, ensuring both privacy protection and invariance to lighting conditions. The model comprises only 326k parameters and achieves real-time inference in just 1.19 milliseconds on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano. Experimental results indicate that RadarPose demonstrates competitive accuracy compared to vision-based models in well-lit environments and significantly outperforms them in low-light or occluded scenarios, highlighting its potential for robust, edge-compatible hand pose estimation. The code and dataset were available at https://github.com/thetuantrinh/UWB-Radar-Hand-Pose-Estimation.git.

title: Unleashing the Potential of Large Language Models for Dynamic Multiobjective Optimization
authors: Min Jiang, Huolong Wu, Zhenzhong Wang, Gary G. Yen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Dynamic multiobjective optimization problems (DMOPs) involve multiple conflicting objectives that change over time. Over the past decade, many dynamic multiobjective optimization algorithms have been developed to effectively track the evolving Pareto optimal solutions. Among these, a promising approach is learning-driven dynamic multiobjective optimization algorithms, which leverage search experience or optimization knowledge to accelerate the evolutionary process. Despite their promising performance, these methods often suffer from low data efficiency and poor model usability. Specifically, they are data-hungry and require extensive strategy design and hyperparameter tuning. To address these challenges, this work introduces a novel approach for DMOPs using large language models (LLMs). Specifically, we reformulate the solution prediction in DMOPs as a time-series prediction problem and guide the LLMs to predict solutions using four carefully crafted prompts. These four types of prompts provide LLMs with different knowledge, enabling distinct reasoning abilities for solution prediction. This prompt-based approach allows LLMs to accurately predict solutions using minimal historical data, without requiring model training or hyperparameter tuning. Experiments on benchmark problems and a real-world application demonstrate the potential of using LLMs to solve DMOPs.

title: Internal Temperature Measurement of Transformers Based on Ultrasonic Sensing and SE-CNN
authors: Yue Sun, Dongxin He, Xinhua Guo
abstract: The internal temperature of oil-immersed transformers serves as a vital indicator for assessing operational performance and longevity. Conventional methods employing embedded temperature sensors have potential safety hazards. Moreover, the structural complexity of transformers introduces uncertainties in ultrasonic propagation paths, rendering traditional ultrasonic inversion algorithms ineffective. To address these challenges, we propose a non-invasive temperature measurement method based on the temperature-dependent properties of ultrasonic waves and a squeeze-and-excitation convolutional neural network (SE-CNN) algorithm. Focusing on a 110kV transformer, an ultrasonic measurement system was developed to capture ultrasonic signals by varying internal temperatures during normal operation. A Butterworth filter was applied to eliminate low-frequency interference in the acquired signals. The SE-CNN algorithm adaptively recalibrates feature channel weights, enabling precise temperature inversion by prioritizing critical signal characteristics. Experimental results demonstrate a remarkable temperature inversion accuracy of 98.75%. By integrating ultrasonic sensing with deep learning, this research establishes a groundbreaking approach for internal temperature monitoring in transformers, offering enhanced safety and reliability compared to traditional sensor-based techniques.

title: Artificial Intelligence Models for Analysing Children’s Emotional Expressions and Colour Use in Digital Drawings
authors: Miguel Ornia-Gomez, Miguel Sánchez-Santillán, MPuerto Paule-Ruiz, Juan Ramón Pérez-Pérez
abstract: This study examines the relationship between preschool children’s emotional expression and colour use in digital drawings produced during an authentic classroom activity. A total of 216 children created drawings and recorded an oral narration in European Spanish. An expert identified, in real time, the emotional expression conveyed by each narration and categorised it by valence as positive, neutral, or negative. To examine the consistency of these labels, Machine Learning models were trained on audio recordings and combined into meta-models. When applied to the Expert dataset of 618 audio-drawing pairs, the best meta-model achieved an accuracy of 0.85 and an F1-score of 0.83, while agreement with the expert labels was moderate, with most disagreements concentrated in the neutral category. Drawings associated with positive emotional expression showed a larger coloured area and a greater number of colours than those associated with negative expression, and a larger coloured area than those associated with neutral expression. Greater use of green was also observed in positive than in negative drawings, although this pattern may partly reflect nature-related topics represented in the drawing.

title: Interference and Overhead Aware Flexible Duplexing for Distributed and Centralized Wireless Networks
authors: Yomali Lokugama, Saman Atapattu, Zhou Zhang
abstract: This letter studies interference-aware flexible duplexing (FlexD) in multi-pair wireless networks under distributed (D-FlexD) and centralized (C-FlexD) coordination. Unlike prior FlexD studies, we explicitly incorporate contention and coordination overhead within each coherence block and analyze the resulting throughput outage and average throughput under full and partial channel state information (CSI). For D-FlexD, closed-form outage and rate expressions are derived using a geometric contention model, while for C-FlexD we obtain order-statistic outage expressions under centralized pair selection. High-SINR analysis reveals a geometry-limited outage floor. Numerical results validate the analysis and identify regimes where half-duplex (HD), FlexD, and full-duplex (FD) are preferred.

title: An Energy-Efficient Lyapunov-Based Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Controller for Electric Vehicles
authors: Hamed Faghihian, Parisa Ansari-Bonab, Arman Sargolzaei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: As electric vehicles (EVs) are increasingly adopted as platforms for connected and automated vehicles (CAVs), enhancing their energy efficiency becomes critical. With the emergence of vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication, cooperative adaptive cruise control (CACC) offers improved traffic flow, safety, and energy efficiency by enabling real-time coordination among EVs. However, conventional CACC algorithms neglected acceleration and regenerative braking dynamics in their implementation. To address this gap, this paper proposes a third-order dynamic model for EVs which has been derived from real-world experimental data. We also propose a novel, practical, and energy-efficient Lyapunov-based CACC controller explicitly designed for EV platoons. The proposed controller is requiring lower control gains while ensuring string stability and energy efficiency. To validate its effectiveness, we conduct both simulation and experimental environments, demonstrating that our approach reduces velocity fluctuations, maintains string stability at lower headway times, and improves energy efficiency of the CACC platoon by up to 38.5% compared to a baseline CACC.

title: Two-Dimensional DOA Estimation Using Augmented Extended Co-Prime Parallel Arrays in Impulse Noise
authors: Jitong Ma, Haibo Xu, Xiao Chen, Jie Wang
abstract: With the rapid development of Internet of Things (IoT), accurate and robust two-dimensional direction of arrival (2-D DOA) estimation has become more and more crucial. This paper addresses the significant challenge of 2-D DOA estimation with high degrees of freedom (DOF) under impulsive noise in practical IoT systems. Firstly, an augmented extended coprime parallel array is proposed by integrating an extended coprime array (ECA) with an augmented unfolded coprime array. The proposed design exploits non-circular signal characteristics, achieving an enhancement in DOF. Furthermore, a generalized versoria function (GVF) is introduced to suppress impulsive noise, and a GVF-based covariance matrix is developed to achieve robust DOA estimation. Simulation results confirm the proposed method achieves higher estimation accuracy and DOF under impulsive noise, making it suitable for reliable IoT systems.

title: Neural Network-Assisted NLoS Channel Identification in Near-Field Environments: A Linear Fractionalization Approach
authors: Dong-Hwan Kim, Jung-Hwan Kim, Hye-Jin Cho, Jong-Ho Lee, Woong-Hee Lee
abstract: This letter introduces a neural network (NN)-assisted non-line-of-sight (NLoS) identification method for near-field environments. Unlike prior methods that depend on raw channel measurements and require high computational complexity, our method introduces a novel channel re-representation that maximizes the learning capability of the NN, referred to as the linear fractional channel, leading to more efficient NLoS identification. The proposed method exploits the fact that the Hankelized matrix of a linear fractional channel becomes rank-1 as both the K-factor and SNR tend to infinity. In a nutshell, we set the input and output of the NN to the singular values of the Hankelized matrix of the linear fractional channel and the one-hot encoding vector, respectively. Numerical results show that the performance of the proposed method has remarkable accuracy compared with existing baseline schemes.

title: Multi-Scale Spatiotemporal-Enhanced Network for Multimodal Traffic Flow Joint Prediction
authors: Xuan Yang, Jiahao Ling, Shimin Gong, Bo Gu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: With growing complexity in urban transportation systems, multimodal traffic flow prediction is essential for coordinating multiple transport modes, optimizing resource allocation, and enhancing urban mobility efficiency. To improve prediction accuracy, it is essential to model cross-modal correlations effectively. However, existing methods in cross-modal correlation modeling overlook two critical issues: 1) Existing cross-modal graph construction methods fail to uncover the latent relationships between nodes based on the travel patterns across different modes; 2) Existing cross-modal feature extraction methods are limited to a single scale and lack comprehensive spatiotemporal representations, resulting in incomplete modeling of cross-modal dependencies. To enhance cross-modal correlation capture, we propose a novel framework named Multi-Scale Cross-Modal Spatiotemporal Enhanced Network (MCSTEN). Specifically, we design a cross-modal graph generator that utilizes tensor decomposition to uncover latent node correlations from peak-time asynchrony features across modes and constructs a cross-modal graph with rich information. Subsequently, we introduce a multi-scale feature extraction block equipped with a scale transformation mechanism to enhance the modeling of cross-modal correlations at different temporal and spatial scales. Finally, an attention-based fusion strategy integrates these multi-scale correlations, adaptively balancing the contribution of each scale. Experimental results on seven real-world multimodal transportation datasets demonstrate that MCSTEN significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in prediction accuracy.

title: Security–Spectral Efficiency Tradeoff in STAR-RIS RSMA: A Max–Min Fairness Framework
authors: Huiyun Xia, Yijie Mao, Sai Xu, Shuai Han, Hongbo Zhu
abstract: Simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (STAR-RISs) enable full-space coverage but also expose wireless transmissions to security from multiple spatial directions. This paper investigates a STAR-RIS-assisted secure RSMA system where both internal and external eavesdroppers may coexist in the transmission and reflection regions. In such a scenario, the RSMA common stream simultaneously serves legitimate users, impairs external eavesdroppers, and avoids assisting internal eavesdroppers, leading to a challenging trade-off between spectral efficiency and confidentiality. To address this issue, we formulate a max–min fairness problem under secrecy constraints and develop an iterative algorithm to jointly optimize transmit beamforming and STAR-RIS phase shifts. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme improves spectral efficiency while maintaining confidentiality.

title: DIR-HSI: Degradation-Invariant Representation Learning for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Boxiang Yang, Ning Chen, Yingbo Fan, Zexi Shi, Haoyuan Zhang, Hui Liu, Run Niu, Shanjun Mao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In recent years, Hyperspectral Image (HSI) classification has achieved remarkable progress in fine-grained surface recognition and intelligent remote sensing analysis. However, real-world degradations—such as sensor noise, compression artifacts, and atmospheric interference—often introduce severe domain shifts, leading to degraded robustness and limited generalization of existing models. To address this challenge, we propose DIR-HSI, a degradation-invariant hyperspectral classification framework designed to enhance feature stability and discriminability under multi-degradation conditions. The framework comprises two key modules: the DIR-FFE (Degradation-Invariant Representation by Fourier-domain Feature Extraction) module, which leverages Fourier-domain feature decomposition to extract discriminative and degradation-insensitive frequency features, and the DIR-CE (Degradation-Invariant Representation Contrastive Enhancement) module, which employs cross-degradation supervised contrastive learning to enforce consistency among same-class representations across different degradation conditions. Together, these components enable effective disentanglement of degradation-sensitive and stable factors, maintaining feature consistency and reinforcing class separability. The key advantage of DIR-HSI lies in its ability to jointly model degradation-invariant and discriminative representations, achieving robust and generalizable performance across complex degradation scenarios. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that DIR-HSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, robustness, and generalization. The code will be available at https://github.com/yangboxiang1207/DIR-HSI.

title: Molecular Nanonetworks: A QoS Aware Adaptive MAC Protocol
authors: Grigorios Karagiannakis, Konstantinos Kantelis, Petros Nicopolitidis, Georgios Papadimitriou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Molecular, Biological, and Multi-Scale Communications
abstract: Molecular nanonetworks are an emerging research area that bridges nanotechnology and biology with the principles of networking. In diffusion-based models, information-carrying molecules move according to the principles of Brownian motion, resulting in the random nature of their propagation through the medium. The presence of multiple transmitters and a single receiver represents a critical communication scenario in molecular nanonetworks, driven by application requirements. In this work, a Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)-based MAC protocol is proposed, achieving high performance and enabling the development of applications that offer Quality of Service (QoS). According to the proposed protocol, each transmitter, prior to releasing information-carrying molecules, sends a control message to the receiver to initiate a connection path. Upon reception, the receiver, acting as a control node, responds with a control message to acknowledge the connection request and adjust the number of information-carrying molecules to be released by the transmitters to prevent receiver saturation caused by the excessive concentration of information molecules. Simulation results indicate that the proposed protocol enhances message detection reliability, improves channel utilization efficiency, and ensures stable QoS performance across varying network conditions. By implementing a TDMA-based MAC protocol and a synchronization process for message exchange, we aim to address these challenges and improve overall system performance while introducing QoS characteristics essential for healthcare applications. The proposed protocol serves as a foundation for a new generation of MAC protocols designed for molecular nanonetworks, capable of adapting to varying transmission distances and transmission power levels within the Internet of Bio-NanoThings (IoBNT) framework.

title: Sine-square addition equations for rectangle fused silica grating
authors: Yuda Chen, Yihan Wang, Wei Jia, Zhisen Huang, Changhe Zhou, Jin Wang
abstract: We presented analytical sine-square-addition equations for rectangle fused silica grating diffraction under second Bragg incidence with unequal refractive indices of three modes. We found that the efficiencies of diffraction orders can be described by the analytical equations consisting of the sine-square summation based on refractive indices differences of any two modes. We matched the coefficient values of analytical equations and compared with the results of Rigorous Coupled Wave Analysis. The results show that the differences between them are less than 3% for TE polarization, and less than 2% for TM polarization. For the first time, these analytical equations reflect the internal physical mechanism in general case where mode refractive indices are not equal, which will provide a powerful tool to obtain a new series of analytical equations for extending grating theory.

title: Routing Strategy for a Multi-layer Virtual Information Defined Satellite Network
authors: Leyan Xu, Yingjian Liu, Yindong Wen, Wei Hou, Zhongwen Guo
abstract: As space communication technology advances at a rapid pace, integrated networks spanning air, space, land, and sea have demonstrated significant potential applications in various fields. However, traditional Transmission Control Protocol/ Internet Protocol (TCP/IP) may not cope well with the high dynamics of satellite networks. Thus, the Virtual Information Defined Satellite Network (VIDSN) architecture is proposed adopting the virtual node strategy. The Pending Interest Table (PIT) and Forwarding Information Base (FIB) are redesigned accordingly, and FIB table is updated through controller polling. To realize efficient data transmission in dynamic satellite network, an appropriate routing strategy is essential. A novel Multi-layer Satellite Routing (MLSR) algorithm is proposed for satellite IoT networks, which adopts a hierarchical routing control architecture to enable scalable and coordinated routing. By exploiting the hierarchical structure of multi-layer satellite networks, the proposed MLSR algorithm effectively supports dynamic topology and traffic variations. Simulation results demonstrate that MLSR comprehensively outperforms baseline methods.

title: Joint Robust Routing, Scheduling, and Load Balancing in Deterministic UAV Rescue Networks: A DFS-PPO Method
authors: Gang Chen, Guofeng Zhao, Zhenzhen Han, Chuan Xu, Shui Yu
abstract: In post-disaster recovery scenarios, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are widely deployed as aerial base stations to ensure deterministic communication services for time-sensitive (TS) flows requiring strict latency and reliability requirements. However, dynamic network topologies may render existing schedules invalid, and newly arriving flows can cause time-slot conflicts with ongoing transmissions, resulting in low resource utilization. To address these challenges, we propose a Depth-First Search-based Proximal Policy Optimization (DFS-PPO) approach for robust data flow routing and scheduling. The proposed method aims to balance link loads while satisfying reliability and latency constraints. A pruned DFS algorithm is used to determine the candidate routing set. To capture the spatial dependencies of all link states in the network, a Graph Attention Network (GAT) is employed to extract the global network state. In addition, a Transformer is adopted to model the nonlinear relationship between candidate routes and the network state. A PPO agent then makes routing decisions based on the high-level representation generated by the Transformer, enabling the model to adapt dynamically to changing network conditions and effectively mitigate bottlenecks. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed DFS-PPO approach significantly outperforms benchmark algorithms in terms of network load balancing, reliability, and the number of successfully scheduled TS flows.

title: 9.4 μ W 285 Samples/s Capacitance to Digital Converter for Large Area Active EWOD Arrays in Thin-Film Transistor Technology
authors: Mauricio Velazquez Lopez, Nikolas Papadopoulos, Francois Berghmans, Kris Myny
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Circuits and Systems
abstract: This work introduces a low-power, linear-in-log capacitance sensing circuit tailored for large-area electrowetting-on-dielectric (EWOD) arrays utilizing thin-film transistor (TFT) technology. By leveraging the intrinsic time constants of the circuit, the proposed approach achieves enhanced sensitivity at low capacitance levels, effectively addressing scenarios where the EWOD electrode capacitance is on par with parasitic capacitances present in the system. Fabricated using a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4.5~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>m Low Temperature Polysilicon (LTPS) TFT process, the circuit demonstrates up to 6-bit resolution, corresponding to 63 distinct measured capacitance levels, and measures capacitance variations as small as 61.3 fF. Two circuit variants are presented: the first, employing a one-transistor–one-resistor (1T1R) input stage, which operates at a peak/mean power consumption of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$16~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>W / <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.0~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>W and occupies <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$324\times 324~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>m2; the second, incorporating a current mirror (CM) input stage, consumes <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$35~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>W / <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$9.4~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>W with an area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$317\times 506~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>m2. Both implementations mark a significant improvement in resolution, sensitivity and power efficiency compared to the current state-of-the-art in TFT technology.

title: Manifold-Based Analysis of P–Q Coupling in Grid-Forming Converters with Current and Voltage Constraints
authors: Ziqian Zhang, Robert Schuerhuber, Lothar Fickert, Guochu Chen
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: This paper presents a visualization of the (θ,E) manifold that governs P–Q coupling in grid-forming converters (GFM). A simplified large-signal model is derived, using the internal voltage magnitude E and phase angle θ as state variables. The model clarifies the coupling among P, Q, E, and θ, and shows how the thresholds of current and voltage limiters determine both the existence and accessibility of stable equilibrium points. By tracing the operating point trajectories on the E–θ manifold, the P–Q coupling surface reveals the mechanism that leads to transient loss of synchronization during abrupt phase jumps. The analysis further explains how limiters modify the dynamic critical phase angle that separates secure and insecure operating regions, and highlights the dual role of small-signal based P–Q decoupling: it increases the admissible phase jump margin but can prolong post-disturbance recovery (the disturbance considered throughout is a permanent grid voltage phase jump) when limiters are active.

title: AS-TEB: An Adaptive and Smooth Timed Elastic Band-Based Path Planner for Mobile Robots
authors: Youcai Shan, Qianqian Wang, Xiaoyu Wang, Jiangfeng Nan, Jie Chen, Qiang Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Conventional Timed Elastic Band (TEB) algorithms often exhibit velocity fluctuations and generate unsmooth trajectories in complex environments, thereby compromising the motion efficiency and control stability of mobile robots. To address these limitations, this paper presents an Adaptive and Smooth TEB algorithm (AS-TEB) with self-regulatory capabilities. First, a curvature-based adaptive sampling strategy is developed, which reduces optimization dimensionality while preserving tracking accuracy and preliminarily enhancing trajectory and velocity continuity. Next, jerk constraints are incorporated to suppress high-frequency velocity fluctuations, thereby improving dynamic smoothness. Furthermore, a velocity-curvature radius constraint is introduced, explicitly linking linear velocity with path curvature and the robot’s minimum turning radius, enabling adaptive speed adjustment and alleviating velocity-curvature mismatches. Extensive simulations and hardware experiments demonstrate that AS-TEB significantly reduces velocity fluctuations, enhances trajectory smoothness, and maintains high motion efficiency compared to conventional TEB and several improved variants, exhibiting strong generality and stability on both differential and Ackermann platforms. This approach offers an efficient and practical solution for local path planning in complex dynamic environments.

title: Quantum Subroutines in Branch-Price-and-Cut for Vehicle Routing
authors: Friedrich Wagner, Frauke Liers
abstract: Motivated by recent progress in quantum hardware and algorithms, researchers have developed quantum heuristics for optimization problems, aiming for advantages over classical methods. To date, quantum hardware is still error-prone and limited in size such that quantum heuristics cannot be scaled to relevant problem sizes and are often outperformed by their classical counterparts. Moreover, if provably optimal solutions are desired, one has to resort to classical exact methods. As, however, quantum technologies may improve considerably in future, we demonstrate in this work how quantum heuristics with limited resources can be integrated in large-scale exact optimization algorithms for NP-hard problems. To this end, we consider vehicle routing as a prototypical NP-hard problem. We model the pricing and separation subproblems arising in a branch-price-and-cut algorithm as quadratic unconstrained binary optimization problems. This allows to use established quantum heuristics like quantum annealing or the quantum approximate optimization algorithm for their solution. A key feature of our algorithm is that it profits not only from the best solution returned by the quantum heuristic but from all solutions below a certain cost threshold, thereby exploiting the inherent randomness is quantum algorithms. Moreover, we reduce the requirements on quantum hardware since the subproblems, which are solved via quantum heuristics, are smaller than the original problem. We provide a proof-of-concept experimental study comparing quantum annealing to simulated annealing and to established classical algorithms in our framework. While our hybrid quantum-classical approach is still outperformed by purely classical methods, our results reveal that both pricing and separation may be well suited for quantum heuristics if quantum hardware improves.

title: Negative Backscatter Differential Phases on SHV radars
authors: V. Melnikov, D. Zrnić, M. Istok
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Most polarimetric weather radars utilize the Simultaneous transmission and reception of Horizontally and Vertically polarized waves (SHV configuration). We show radar data exhibiting negative differential phases upon scattering, which is unusual for generally horizontally oriented Rayleigh scatterers. This effect is explained by considering interference of the backscattered primary and depolarized waves inherent to SHV radars.

title: SDFRF-Net: Spatial Diversity Fine-Grained Representation and Fusion Network
authors: Yuelan Feng, Donglin Jing
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: In remote sensing images, due to the significant changes in target scale, arbitrary orientation and high aspect ratio, achieving precise detection of rotating targets still faces huge challenges. Although existing methods have made progress in multi-scale feature fusion or direction-aware design, they usually treat scale and direction as independent problems, ignoring their intrinsic coupling relationship. This fragmented modeling leads to spatial misalignment problems during the detection process, especially for slender targets such as cargo ships and passenger ships, where the accurate estimation of their directions highly depends on the precise extraction of scale features. To solve the above problems, this paper proposes SDFRF-Net. Specifically, we first design the spatial diversity dynamic fusion module FRRD, which adopts stacked hollow small convolution kernels, integrates Ghost linear transformation and spatial attention, and dynamic weighted fusion to extract fine-grained scale features. Secondly, an anchor box is designed to guide the deformable convolutional module GMP. The directional anchor box guides the rotation of the convolutional kernel and equiinterval sampling to adapt to the target shape. Finally, the equilibrium loss Function (LEC) is designed to enhance the model’s attention to small gradient samples by balancing the L1 loss and improve the positioning accuracy. We conducted extensive experiments on the DOTA and HRSC2016 datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of the model we proposed.

title: Digital Twins in Reverse Engineering of a Modular AC Contactors
authors: V. A. Zakharov, S. P. Ivanova
abstract: The article discusses the application of digital twin technology in the design of modular AC contactors using reverse engineering. The conceptual structure of a digital twin for a low-voltage control device is considered. It is noted that the basis of any digital twin is a computer model, i.e. a set of mathematical and computer analogues of the device being designed. It is concluded that one of the main tasks in reverse engineering of modular AC contactors is to optimize the design of the device prototype in order to improve it in accordance with the requirements of the technical specifications. In particular, it was found that mathematical modelling of modular contactors based on the theoretical principles of electromagnetic and thermal field theory can significantly simplify optimization and parametric calculations in the development of new products. To illustrate the application of the proposed methodology, a mathematical model of the electromagnetic field of an AC electromagnet of an S-shape was developed in the FEMM 4.2 finite element analysis program for physical fields. The advantages of the model approach in the parametric calculation of static traction characteristics are shown. The dependence of the thickness of the electromagnet's magnetic circuit and its traction forces at different air gap values has been established. The results of the research can be applied in the development of digital twins of low-voltage control equipment using reverse engineering techniques.

title: A Hybrid Short-Term Photovoltaic Power Forecasting Model Integrating Physical Mechanism Constraints and Weather Scenario Clustering
authors: Zhenpeng Guo, Da Wang, Yunpeng Jia
abstract: To address the high non-stationarity of photovoltaic (PV) power sequences and the lack of physical constraints in existing pure data-driven models, this paper proposes a combined forecasting model integrating feature decomposition-reconstruction and a Physics-Informed Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (PI-KAN). First, Successive Variational Mode Decomposition (SVMD) is introduced to adaptively decompose the power sequence. Sample Entropy (SE) is then utilized to reconstruct components into high- and low-frequency features, combined with the Maximal Information Coefficient (MIC) to screen key meteorological factors. Second, the Elkan K-means algorithm clusters historical data into weather scenarios to enhance adaptability to different fluctuation characteristics. Furthermore, a PI-KAN forecasting model is constructed, embedding the residual of the Single Diode Model (SDM) into the loss function to correct prediction deviations through a dual data-mechanism drive. Finally, the Dung Beetle Optimizer (DBO) adaptively optimizes the network structure and physical weights. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms contrast models under various weather types, reducing the E<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">RMSE</inf> and E<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">MAE</inf> by 12.4% and 14.7%, respectively, while improving the coefficient of determination R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> by 3.84%.

title: Method for Manipulator Kinematic Parameters Identification Using Three-Axis Accelerometer
authors: Ivan M. Grigorev, Dmitry A. Yukhimets
abstract: Generally, nominal (specified in documentation) parameters of the manipulator kinematics differ slightly from the real ones, which negatively affects the accuracy of positioning the manipulator end link (flange) in Cartesian coordinates. The article proposes a method for robotic manipulators for some real kinematic parameters identification using a microelectromechanical three-axis accelerometer mounted on its flange and used in inclinometer mode. In several static manipulator configurations, the accelerometer measures the free fall acceleration vector, and the positions of the manipulator joints are recorded. After that, using numerical optimization, real kinematic parameters of the manipulator are adjusted so as to reconcile the accelerometer measurements and the recorded joints positions. Increasing the accuracy of the method is achieved due to the fact that even an uncalibrated consumer-grade accelerometer is capable of measuring tilt with an accuracy of hundredths of a degree, provided that only small angles are measured, and most manipulators, even using nominal kinematic parameters, are able to move the flange with a constant orientation with an error small enough for such measurements. The results of mathematical simulation confirm the workability of the method. In addition, identifiability of the parameters is considered, and the effect of identified parameters on increasing accuracy of the manipulator is evaluated.

title: Secure Off-chain Payment Protocol from Ciphertext Unlinkable Autonomous Path Proxy Re-encryption in Blockchain
authors: Yile Chen, Jun Zhou, Zhenfu Cao, Xiaolei Dong, Kim-Kwang Raymond Choo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Secure off-chain payment plays an important role in blockchain ecosystems, requiring atomicity guarantees where either all payment channels update their balances or none do. While the Lightning Network achieves this through multi-hop Hash Time-Lock Contracts (HTLCs), a critical flaw persists. Specifically, contract conditions (e.g., hash preimages) are transmitted in plaintext across payment paths. Consequently, malicious intermediate nodes can launch interception attacks by claiming funds from predecessors without releasing the corresponding coins to successors. To mitigate this risk, we propose a secure off-chain payment protocol. Firstly, a new cryptographic primitive, namely ciphertext unlinkable autonomous path proxy re-encryption scheme (CUAP-PRE), is proposed. Unlike traditional multi-hop proxy re-encryption, it prevents malicious nodes by enabling the delegator to designate all delegatees he trusts. In addition, ciphertext unlinkability resists homology inference attacks and delegation path tracing to ensure anonymity. Building on CUAP-PRE, a secure off-chain payment protocol (SOCP) in blockchain is designed with an enhanced multi-hop Hash Time-Lock Contract of the Payment-Channel Network (PCN). The receiver at the end of one path of payment channel can control the decryption rights of payment condition in multi-hop delegation manner with reversed order, to unlock the corresponding bitcoins on hold. We then present formal security proofs to demonstrate that the proposed CUAP-PRE and SOCP are postcompromise secure under the Decisional Bilinear Diffie-Hellman (DBDH) assumption in the random oracle model. Comprehensive evaluations also demonstrate the effectiveness and practicability of our proposal.

title: Reducing the physical effort of wearing a walking boot using a passive hip exosuit through enforced exploration training
authors: Inigo Sanz-Pena, Sabrina Sullivan, Prakyath Kantharaju, Jaeha Yang, Dimuthu K. Arachchige, Giuk Lee, Myunghee Kim
abstract: Background: Ankle injuries can be detrimental due to their long- recovery times. A walking boot (WB) is the most prescribed treatment. However, they disrupt gait biomechanics, increasing the metabolic cost of walking and the risk of further injuries. Research question: Does using a hip exosuit minimize the adverse effects of wearing a WB, improving gait spatio-temporal parameters, and reduce energy expenditure? Methods: We investigated the effects of a passive hip exosuit and enforced exploration training in users wearing a WB. Subjects wore a WB to simulate the effects of ankle injuries during recovery. Results: The results indicate the benefits of wearing the hip exosuit and training. Metabolic cost reductions of 6.2 ± 1.5% (p < 0.001) between the post training and pre-training and 3.5 ± 2.8% (p = 0.03) between the post training and no exosuit conditions were found. Training with the exosuit resulted in positive gait modifications in patients with ankle injuries associated with gait retraining. The effects of enforced exploration training in gait kinematics wearing the exosuit resulted in an increased maximum hip flexion of 6.04 ± 3.56° and 6.59 ± 5.16° for the boot and free leg, respectively, compared to not wearing the exosuit. Spatiotemporal parameter modifications were adopted after training, resulting in metabolic reductions. Some subjects varied their step frequency, while others varied their step length and width. Significance: The outcomes from this study show the potential benefits that hip exosuits could have in clinical sports rehabilitation of ankle injuries wearing a WB.

title: A Need for Indigenous Database Accelerator Card: Financial and Strategic Applications
authors: Solaimurugan Vellaipandiyan, Parth Jaju, Mridula Kurne, D Ethirajan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computation-Based Sensor Application (ICAIQSA)
abstract: Modern Online Transaction Processing (OLTP) workloads require ultra-low latency and high throughput under strict ACID guarantees. At Alibaba, for instance, the database infrastructure handled $5 \times 10^{\wedge} 5$ sales transactions per second while sustaining sub-millisecond latency [1], [2]. The traditional CPUcentric architecture of relational database management systems (RDBMS) like PostgreSQL cannot keep up with the increasing amount of storage space required for dead tuples from multiple versions of an item that have been added or deleted in a database. This bloat necessitates the need for costly vacuuming to reclaim space used for these items [3]–[5]. Furthermore, with the emerging CPU memory wall [6], performance scaling will continue to be limited. One alternative to current RDBMS architectures is the use of field-programmable gate array (FPGA) technology, which provides the ability to achieve deterministic, customisable, fine-grained parallelism [8] that enables the implementation of transaction pipelines specific to the needs of an application. FPGAs also permit data lookup by using on-chip RAM. Most preceding and future FPGA-DB frameworks (DAnA, BionicDB, SQL2FPGA) [10–12] have shown promising results; however, they face some common challenges, including the limitations of available PCIe/memory bandwidth, the overhead of partial reconfiguration, and integration with PostgreSQL. There is merit in continuing research on HBM FPGAs, FPGA-on-DIMM architecture, and HLS development frameworks [8]. We would advocate for the development of an open-source, PostgreSQL-native stack of FPGA-based OLTP acceleration to enable these benefits to be realised and sustained, with respect to the system’s performance on a modern scale. Given India’s reliance on massive-scale digital platforms such as Aadhaar, IRCTC, and UPI—handling tens of billions of secure transactions monthly—the need for sovereign, high-performance acceleration infrastructure is both urgent and strategic.

title: A Sequential In-Process Energy Recovery Topology for Multistage Reluctance Electromagnetic Launchers
authors: Qichao Wang, Kedong Zhou, Ye Lu
abstract: The performance of electromagnetic launch (EML) systems is generally evaluated in terms of two key parameters: the velocity of the projectile and the overall energy utilization efficiency. However, existing multistage EML circuits are constrained by their topology, which limits the timely reutilization of residual energy and thereby restricts further improvements in both projectile velocity and system efficiency. To address this issue, this study proposes a sequential in-process energy recovery topology (SIPERT). The proposed circuit topology is capable of collecting the residual energy remaining in the circuit after the completion of acceleration in each stage and reutilizing this energy in the subsequent stages of the launch process. Specifically, once the upstream coil conduction is completed, the induced electromotive force is used to recharge the downstream capacitor, thereby both accelerating the attenuation of ineffective freewheeling current and increasing the initial voltage of the subsequent capacitor. The fundamental principle of SIPERT is elucidated in detail, and its performance is validated by experiments on a dedicated test platform. Comparative simulations are further conducted against conventional, bridge-type, and final-stage recovery circuits. The results demonstrate that, relative to the bridge-type circuit, SIPERT achieves a 10.02% increase in projectile velocity and a 14.08% improvement in system efficiency. These findings provide valuable insights for the future design and optimization of EML systems.

title:
CNT/IGZO CFET Circuits Based on Top-Gate IGZO-FETs With Enhanced Hydrogen Resistance and Thermal Stability Using Y
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Passivation
authors: Yijia Fan, Jianshi Tang, Yijun Li, Yiwei Du, Yanbo Su, Zhidong Tang, Yibei Zhang, Ran An, Dong Wu, Bin Gao, He Qian, Huaqiang Wu
abstract: In this work, we report a demonstration of complementary field-effect transistor (CFET) and circuits with p-type carbon nanotube field-effect transistor (CNT-FET) stacked on n-type InGaZnO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> field-effect transistor (IGZO-FET) at a low thermal budget of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$250~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C. To address the issues of hydrogen resistance and thermal stability faced by top-gate IGZO-FETs, a Y<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> passivation layer on Indium Gallium Zinc Oxide (IGZO) was developed for the first time, which yielded <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gt 100\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> reduction in the threshold voltage shift (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>V<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {th}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> during the subsequent atomic layer deposition (ALD). This enabled the fabrication of enhancement-mode top-gate IGZO-FETs with drastically enhanced hydrogen resistance. Moreover, by engineering the top-gate device structure and air annealing, the high on/off ratio was preserved. Furthermore, p-type back-gate CNT-FETs were fabricated on top to fulfill a backend-of-the-line (BEOL)-compatible CNT/IGZO CFET with symmetric P/NFET performance. CFET logic gates and ring oscillators (ROs) with sub-50 ns stage delay at micrometer-scale channel length were also demonstrated. The novel passivation method developed in this work could enable the fabrication of high-performance and high-reliability CNT/IGZO-based CFET and circuits for future 3-D integration.

title: Computer Model-Based Load Equivalenting for Electric Motor of Rolling Mill
authors: Roman A. Lisovsky, Stanislav S. Voronin, Boris M. Loginov, Vadim R. Khramshin
abstract: Expanding the product ranges of rolling mills through the production of sheets from difficult-to-form steels requires calculating equivalent motor loads (moment or current equivalenting). The deployment of digital technology facilitates solving this problem using computer models. This paper studies such a solution for the electric drives of the horizontal stand of the 5000 plate mill. The authors rationalize the two-stage procedure for equivalent load parameters that includes calculations using the developed models during the first stage and testing during rolling during the second stage. To solve the first problem, a calculation model for equivalent current using speed and load diagrams was developed in Matlab Simulink. The use of the integrated square current as the diagnostic criterion has been justified for the assessment of the thermal condition of the motor during a random interval of time. The authors proposed analytical dependencies for the calculation of this parameter during the operation of the electric drive in the constant excitation flux mode and in the reduced flux mode. The developed model was used to calculate the integrated square current and equivalent current for 7 reverse rolling passes. The authors deem the simultaneous analysis of these parameters feasible. To summarize the results, a comparison of equivalent currents calculated using the proposed procedure and those obtained experimentally with a coordinate observer has been performed. The precision of the results is satisfactory. Due to this, the developed equivalenting procedure can be recommended for the thermal condition analysis of rolling mill motors when adopting new rolling profiles.

title: Digital Twin and Native-AI Empowered 6G Networks: Architectures, Applications, Evaluation, and Open Challenges
authors: Yassir Al-Karawi, Hussein Shakor Mogheer, Khamees Khalaf Hasan, Ahmed Hussein Dawwd
abstract: To support these new 6G requirements of high levels of automation, intelligence and reliability, Digital Twins (DTs) will be the base layer to allow for Predictive Assurance, Risk Aware Orchestration, and 'what if' analysis for all layers of the RAN, Edge, and Core. At the same time, as Native Artificial Intelligence (Native-AI), Machine Learning (ML) will be integrated into Control Loops and Data Paths, which will represent an evolution from "AI For Networks" to "AI In Networks," subject to explicit Budget Constraints and Operational Guardrails. The authors make four contributions to this research: (i) A Standards Aligned Reference Architecture, that enables DT Instances to be connected to Native-AI Services through E2/A1/O1 Interfaces; (ii) A Budget Aware Control Framework with Shadow Testing, Canary Deployment, and Automated Rollback; (iii) Case Studies demonstrating SLA Assurance for eMBB/URLLC and Energy Aware Operation using Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS); and (iv) A Security Posture that includes post quantum cryptography, zero trust principles, and complete artefact provenance. The authors show in their extensive emulations a reduction in SLA risk of 30-38%, while keeping control overhead at less than 5%, and 18-24% radio unit energy saving with minimal impact on throughput. They also describe Operator Considerations and highlight Interoperability and Reproducibility Challenges and present a Roadmap towards certified DT + Native-AI operational stacks by 2030-2035.

title: Big-Data-Driven Pipeline for Industry 4.0 Project Team Formation Based on a Modified Shapley Value
authors: Andrey Zaytsev, Maxim Zavrazhnov, Alina Furtatova
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of optimal project team formation under budget constraints and individual rationality requirements specific to the Russian industrial sector. A unified software-analytical pipeline is proposed that transforms raw heterogeneous data from Jira-like systems into prescriptive team compositions. The pipeline employs a robust ETL process to compute a 7-dimensional competence vector (7×S-Score) for each candidate via Z-normalization and weighted summation. A novel heuristic search algorithm (SmartCoalitionFinder) combines deterministic greedy search and guided Monte-Carlo sampling to solve the NP-hard coalition selection problem for n > 30 employees in sub-second time, avoiding combinatorial explosion. A modified Shapley value is introduced that enforces individual rationality (≥75% of market salary, complying with Russian Labour Code Article 129) and strict budget balance via a three-step redistribution mechanism. Validation on a processed Kaggle Jira dataset (6,781 employees, >700,000 logs) augmented with statistically correlated salary data demonstrates a +19.7% increase in mean team value and a 78% reduction in variance compared to random staffing (p < 0.001, 1000 Monte Carlo runs). The system generates auditable outputs (CSV, JSON) deployable within Russian ERP/MES ecosystems such as 1C and KAMAZ-MES. The proposed approach extends Big Data analytics from equipment prognostics to human capital management under the specific economic and legal constraints of Industry 4.0.

title: Short-term Photovoltaic Power Prediction Based on Multi-modal Decomposition and Adaptive Optimization
authors: Guanyu Meng
abstract: In this study, a hybrid modeling framework combining multi-dimensional preprocessing, decomposition technology and parameter optimization is proposed for short-term photovoltaic power prediction to cope with the randomness, volatility and non-stationarity of its output and improve the prediction accuracy. Firstly, the outliers are detected and marked based on the 3σ criterion, and the missing items in the data are filled by the adjacent mean interpolation method. The key meteorological features are screened by combining the Pearson correlation coefficient and the Kendall rank correlation coefficient, and the high-quality input data are obtained by normalization. Secondly, the original non-stationary power series and Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) are decomposed into multiple stationary intrinsic mode functions (IMFs) by using Complete Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition with Adaptive Noise (CEEMDAN) technology to reduce the modeling complexity. Subsequently, an independent CNN-GRU sub-model is constructed for each IMF component, and the PSO algorithm is used to adaptively optimize the hyperparameters of each sub-model. Finally, the final photovoltaic power value is obtained by reconstructing the prediction results of each component. Experiments based on a photovoltaic power station in Jilin show that the normalized root mean square error (RMSE) of the proposed model is 0.0947, and the correlation coefficient (R) is 0.9545. The ablation experiment verifies the synergistic effect of decomposition and parameter optimization. The framework provides an efficient solution for high-precision prediction of photovoltaic power, and has practical engineering application value.

title: Enhancing Information Integrity and Reliability in Energy Telemechanical Systems via Distributed Intelligence Framework
authors: Aung Kyaw Myo, Evgeni M. Portnov, Kugoev Ivan Aleksandrovich
abstract: Telemechanical systems, essential for monitoring and controlling distributed energy facilities, face challenges in reliability, data integrity, and real-time processing when using basic PLC architectures. This paper proposes a distributed intelligence approach with intelligent modules that perform local data acquisition, preprocessing, timestamping, and validation, reducing central processor load and communication overhead. Key innovations include bi-impulse conditionally correlational coding for superior noise immunity, segregation of operative and non-operative data flows, and integration with protocols like DNP3 or HDLC over Modbus limitations. Calculations demonstrate compliance with GOST 26.205 standards, achieving MTBF over 18,000 hours, command distortion probability below 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-9</sup>, and efficient channel utilization under 18% load even on low-speed links. These advancements enable scalable SCADA integration for modern AMI and fault analysis, significantly boosting system performance and fault tolerance.

title: Noise Impact on System Identification Using Genetic Programming-Based Symbolic Regression
authors: Lele Zhang
abstract: System identification using genetic programming-based symbolic regression has emerged as a powerful approach for discovering interpretable mathematical models of dynamic systems directly from data. However, its performance in the presence of ubiquitous measurement noise - a critical real-world constraint - remains insufficiently quantified. This paper rigorously investigates the impact of noise on the genetic programming algorithm when it is used to solve dynamic system identification problems. We apply the genetic programming algorithm to reconstruct the governing equations of the Lorenz system from data corrupted with varying levels of Gaussian noise. Experimental results demonstrate that, while model accuracy degrades with increasing noise intensity, genetic programming consistently and reliably extracts the core structural form of the underlying system. The analysis reveals the inherent trade-off between model complexity and accuracy under noisy conditions, visualized via Pareto fronts. Our findings confirm that genetic programming offers notable robustness for structural identification, successfully discerning the essential system dynamics amidst imperfect data.

title: Decision Support Algorithm for Selecting Model Classes
authors: Vadim Tynchenko, Anastasia Kozlova, Svetlana Pchelintseva
abstract: The paper addresses the problem of a justified choice of an approach to modeling production processes in the context of the active development of the smart manufacturing concept and the implementation of Industry 4.0 technologies. The relevance of the study is driven by the significant complexity of modern production systems, which renders the traditional intuitive selection of modeling methods ineffective and creates risks of using inadequate analytical tools. The primary objective of the article is the development of a formalized method based on a systematic analysis of management goals, decision-making levels, and the characteristics of a specific production system. A proposed algorithm ensures the alignment of management tasks with the specific capabilities of discrete-event, agent-based, system dynamics, and hybrid models. The method includes a sequence of five stages: from initial task identification to quantitative justification of the choice using the weighted sum model. The developed system of criteria considers parameters such as the level of detail, computational complexity, data requirements, and the possibility of integration with the Industrial Internet of Things. The obtained results are presented in the form of structured mapping tables for strategic, tactical, and operational management levels. The proposed approach allows for a reduction in computational costs and an increase in modeling accuracy, providing a methodological basis for designing digital twins.

title: Analysis of SLM Applicability to Prevent Information Leaks Using Business Process Optimization Tools on the PIX RPA Example
authors: Lyudmila Gadasina, Sergey Lapshin
abstract: The article discusses the issue of confidential information leakage when using robotic process automation (RPA) technologies to automate some business processes. This issue is relevant due to the lack of strict security measures in the development of RPA solutions, which can result in the unauthorized dissemination of trade secrets, personal data and other sensitive information. The goal of this work is to develop and test a method for preventing leaks by integrating a system that verifies the data intended to transfer outside the company using small language models into RPA workflows. The PIX Studio development platform was used for the implementation and testing of the proposed method. In the course of our research, we proposed an architecture for a secure RPA solution. We developed a special test set to detect confidential information in heterogeneous corporate data. To evaluate the effectiveness of our solution, we experimentally tested five small language models using precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. The Gemma and Mistral models demonstrated the best results, confirming the feasibility of using small language models for local data analysis without transferring information to external servers. The practical significance of this work lies in the proposal of a ready-made leak control mechanism that can be integrated into RPA platforms. This corresponds with the current trends in intelligent automation and the implementation of DevSecOps (development, security, operations) principles in corporate processes.

title: Enabling Deterministic 6G Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Through Predictive and Semantic O-RAN Intelligence
authors: Antoine Hitayezu, Jui-Tang Wang
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: The emergence of 6 G networks introduces unprecedented demands for deterministic, ultra-reliable, and low-latency communication (URLLC) across diverse service domains such as autonomous mobility, industrial automation, and immersive applications. Achieving these requirements within the disaggregated and multi-vendor Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN) architecture remains a major challenge, particularly due to the dynamic nature of radio conditions and transport-layer variability. This paper proposes a proactive, AI-native latency control framework for O-RAN-based 5G/6G networks, targeting deterministic ultrareliable low-latency communication (URLLC) under heterogeneous traffic conditions. Unlike prior reactive or slice-static approaches, the proposed architecture embeds predictive latency modeling and semantic policy reasoning directly within the near-real-time RAN Intelligent Controller (RIC), enabling anticipation and mitigation of end-to-end delay violations before they occur. Through an experimental testbed based on the O-RAN Software Community (OSC) stack, srsRAN, and an open 5G core, the proposed approach demonstrates a consistent reduction in tail latency and hybrid automatic repeat request (HARQ) deadline violations compared to conventional O-RAN scheduling. The results confirm that embedding network intelligence and semantic control within the O-RAN control loop can achieve reliable sub-5 ms communication performance, offering a practical pathway toward AI-native, latency-deterministic 6 G systems.

title: CoMAEA: A Collision-Avoiding Multi-Agent Evolutionary Algorithm for Coverage Path Planning_supp1-3665409.pdf
authors: Ting Huang
abstract: Efficient and safe path planning for multiple autonomous agents, such as unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), is essential in many real-world applications. The objective is to coordinate these agents to cover a target area with minimal cost. However, most existing multi-agent coverage path planning (MACPP) methods rely on purely spatial planning and neglect spatio-temporal interactions among agents, which often result in unsafe paths and a high risk of inter-agent collisions during execution. To address this challenge, we propose a new problem formulation, termed collision-aware MACPP (CaMACPP), which incorporates both spatial coverage efficiency and collision avoidance. To solve this problem, we introduce an efficient multi-agent evolutionary algorithm to minimize costs while avoiding collisions. The algorithm first introduces a trapezoidal decomposition strategy to partition complex environments into convex subareas. Then, a cooperative evolutionary algorithm is designed to optimize the inter-area visiting orders for multiple agents and the intra-area coverage modes assigned to each agent. Next, a spatio-temporal collision avoidance mechanism is incorporated to detect and resolve conflicts between agent paths. The proposed algorithm is extensively evaluated against the state-of-the-art MACPP methods to demonstrate its effectiveness in generating collision-free paths with minimal total cost. The results show that it achieves zero collisions in all tested scenarios, reduces the average path length by 15.9%, and decreases coverage overlap by 58.1%.
