title: An Efficient LiDAR-Camera Fusion Network for Multi-Class 3D Dynamic Object Detection and Trajectory Prediction
authors: Yushen He, Lei Zhao, Tianchen Deng, Zipeng Fang, Weidong Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Service mobile robots are often required to avoid dynamic objects while performing their tasks, but they usually have only limited computational resources. To further advance the practical application of service robots in complex dynamic environments, we propose an efficient multi-modal framework for 3D object detection and trajectory prediction, which synergistically integrates LiDAR and camera inputs to achieve real-time perception of pedestrians, vehicles, and riders in 3D space. The framework incorporates two novel models: 1) a Unified modality detector with Mamba and Transformer (UniMT) for object detection, which achieves high-accuracy object detection with fast inference speed, and 2) a Reference Trajectory-based Multi-Class Transformer (RTMCT) for efficient and diverse trajectory prediction of multi-class objects with flexible-length trajectories. Evaluations on the CODa benchmark demonstrate that our method outperforms existing ones in both detection (+3.71% in mAP) and trajectory prediction (-0.408m in minADE<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sub> of pedestrians) metrics. Furthermore, on the challenging nuScenes detection benchmark, our detection model achieves competitive performance among LiDAR-camera fusion methods, with a mAP of 72.7% and NDS of 75.3%. Remarkably, the system demonstrates exceptional generalizability and practical deployment potential. When transferred and implemented on a wheelchair robot with an entry-level NVIDIA RTX 3060 GPU, it achieves real-time inference at 13.9 frames per second (FPS) with satisfactory accuracy across different environments. To facilitate reproducibility and practical deployment, we release the related code of the method at https://github.com/TossherO/3D Perception and its ROS inference version at https://github.com/TossherO/ros packages.

title: Fundamental Rate-CRB Tradeoff in MIMO Downlink Systems for Integrated Sensing and Communication
authors: Gyeongmin Bae, Daeyoung Park
abstract: This letter studies the joint beamforming design for Multi-user MIMO (MU-MIMO) downlink integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, focusing on characterizing and achieving the fundamental tradeoff between communication throughput and sensing performance. We specifically address the complex scenario of extended target models, which means the base station (BS) estimates the target response matrix. Sensing performance is rigorously evaluated using both the trace and the determinant of the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) matrix, going beyond prior analyses limited to single-antenna users. We formulate a highly non-convex optimization problem integrating weighted sum rate maximization with these CRB metrics. To efficiently solve this challenging problem, we propose a novel iterative framework combining the Weighted Minimum Mean Squared Error (WMMSE) algorithm with the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM), deriving efficient semi-closed-form proximal operator solutions based on singular value decomposition. Furthermore, we introduce WMMSE-PS (power splitting) as a low-complexity baseline derived from the orthogonality property of the optimal beamformers. Numerical results validate the proposed WMMSE-ADMM approach, demonstrating its capability to accurately trace the Rate-CRB tradeoff curve and achieve empirically near-optimal CRB-rate tradeoffs across the entire performance region, significantly outperforming the WMMSE-PS scheme, especially in sensing-dominant regimes.

title: Infrared and Visible Ship Image Fusion Based on Adaptive Cross-modal Feature Interaction and Multi-scale Frequency-domain Transformation
authors: Limin Guo, Yuwu Wang, Xiaohai Zhou, Yue Wu, Guifu Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Infrared and visible image fusion serves as a pivotal technique for all-weather ship surveillance in maritime security applications, which can compensate for the inherent deficiencies of single-modal imaging under low-illumination and complex sea-state conditions. Nevertheless, existing fusion algorithms confront three core bottlenecks in coastal ship scenarios: first, insufficient robust feature representation capability against complex sea-sky backgrounds and multi-scale ship targets; second, inefficient cross-modal feature interaction between infrared and visible modalities; third, weak scene adaptability arising from static weight allocation in multi-scale frequency-domain fusion. To address these issues, this paper proposes an infrared and visible ship image fusion algorithm (AMTFuse) based on adaptive cross-modal feature interaction and multi-scale frequency-domain transformation, and constructs the IVSHIP dataset dedicated to ship image fusion. AMTFuse adopts an end-to-end framework and consists of three core modules: firstly, a contrastive learning-based feature enhancement network (CLFE) is developed, which mitigates small-target feature missing and insufficient modal discriminability in complex maritime scenarios via multi-scale feature distillation paths, cross-modal contrastive learning, and enhanced spatial attention; secondly, an adaptive cross-modal feature interaction module (ACMF) is devised, which breaks through the limitations of static interaction and poor scene adaptability in existing cross-modal Transformers by virtue of modal-aware gating, multi-level feature alignment, and dynamic attention updating; finally, a multi-scale dynamic frequency-domain fusion module (MSDF) is established, which implements multi-scale frequency-domain decomposition and content-aware dynamic weight fusion at the deep feature level, remedying the defects of spatial-frequency decoupling and static weighting in conventional frequency-domain methods. Experimental results on the self-built IVSHIP dataset as well as public MSRS and TNO datasets demonstrate that AMTFuse significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art fusion algorithms in both quantitative metrics and visual effects. Furthermore, this paper validates that the proposed algorithm can be applied to boost the performance of high-level computer vision tasks (i.e., object detection).

title: Dispatcher-Oriented Digitalization of Maintenance Processes for Railway Signalling and Telecommunication Systems
authors: Shakhnoza B. Otakulova, Natalya V. Yaronova
abstract: Maintenance of railway automation and telemechanics devices in Uzbekistan Railways is regulated by NSH-01 and relies on paper logbooks (DU-46, SHU forms), which increases manual workload, delays reporting, and limits analytics. This paper proposes an e-dispatcher platform that unifies real-time monitoring with electronic document management across the full maintenance cycle: event registration, diagnostics, work-order creation, executor assignment, execution tracking, electronic act/report with audit trail, and centralized storage. A data-integration layer normalizes heterogeneous monitoring streams, while analytics modules estimate reliability and detect recurrent failures. The solution significantly reduces time losses, human-factor errors, and improves dispatcher supervision and overall traffic safety. From a Smart Industry perspective, the proposed solution follows an Industrial Internet of Things (IoT)–oriented approach to maintenance digitalization, enabling centralized dispatcher supervision and data-driven analysis of operational reliability. The obtained results demonstrate the potential of the platform to reduce manual workload, improve transparency of maintenance processes, and create a structured data basis for reliability assessment and further optimization.

title: Designing a UWB Printed Flag-Shaped Antenna with Circular Polarization Using Characteristic Mode Analysis
authors: Van Lam Pham, Valerii P. Kosteletskii
abstract: This paper presents a compact UWB circularly polarized (CP) printed flag-shaped antenna designed for wireless communication systems in industrial robotics. The antenna design is based on Characteristic Mode Analysis (CMA), which enables the identification of dominant modes and the conditions required for CP generation. The results of the modal analysis are employed to predict the operating frequency bands, followed by full-wave electromagnetic simulation to validate the obtained findings. The proposed antenna achieves an impedance matching bandwidth with a 10-dB reflection coefficient over the frequency range of 2.32–5.41 GHz and maintains an axial ratio (AR) of less than 3 dB across the 3–5 GHz band. The gain in the direction normal to the radiator plane ranges from 1.5 to 3.3 dBi, exceeding 3 dBi over most of the CP bandwidth. The radiation pattern exhibits a bidirectional characteristic along the z-axis. The simulation results confirm the feasibility of deploying the proposed antenna in mobile robotic systems.

title: Multi-Pass UAV LiDAR Point Cloud Densification Using MLS/RBF Interpolation
authors: Vladimir S. Gabov, Alexey V. Zinkevich
abstract: The use of lidars on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) makes it possible to obtain high-precision 3D point clouds, however, in real missions the data often turns out to be sparse and heterogeneous in density, which worsens the quality of subsequent segmentation and 3D reconstruction, which is important for digital counterparts. In this paper, we propose and investigate a method for compacting a cloud of UAV LiDAR points over the Amur River shoreline based on multi-pass surveys along various tacks and data accumulation with geometric consistency control relative to the reference surface. The reference surface is formed along the selected passage by interpolating Moving Least Squares (MLS) and Radial Basis Functions (RBF) on a regular grid with a step of 0.01 m. The points of the subsequent passes are compared with the reference surface; the points, the distance to which does not exceed 0.5 m, are accepted and included in the final cloud, providing filtering of inconsistent measurements. Experimental evaluation shows comparable efficiency of MLS and RBF: MLS reached MR = 15.71%, RMSD = 2.7600 m and density increase 3498 times; RBF — MR = 14.87%, RMSD = 3.1534 m and density increase 3663 times. The proposed approach provides an increase in cloud density by more than 3,500 times while maintaining geometric accuracy, creating an informative basis for automated segmentation and 3D reconstruction.

title: The Use of Artificial Intelligence as an Expert
authors: Oleg Gorshkov, Vladimir Postnikov, Zhanna Mingaleva
abstract: This paper explores the potential of applying artificial intelligence technologies as expert systems across various industries in order to address workforce shortages. Different types of intelligent systems are examined, and the benefits and risks associated with integrating modern technologies for conducting various forms of assessment are discussed. Particular emphasis is placed on the importance of complying with legal regulations, data security requirements, and ethical considerations. To ensure efficient processing of large volumes of incoming requests and data, the paper proposes a solution that combines the "queue" and "observer" design patterns, enabling flexible distribution of computational load and resilience to activity spikes when large amounts of information must be processed. The solution is based on a multi-agent system, which makes it possible to combine multiple AI agents and improve the accuracy of expert evaluation.

title: Toward the Use of a Block of Two Planar Spiral Solenoids in High-temperature Magnetostrictive Sensors Based on Torsional Waves
authors: A. V. Knyazkov, Yu. N. Slesarev, A. A. Vorontsov
abstract: Article presents the results of a study of the output voltage in the acoustic paths of magnetostrictive linear displacement sensors using flat spiral solenoids. The latter are promising alternative to traditional cylindrical coils due to their compact design, increased manufacturability, and the possibility of integration on a printed circuit board together with signal processing circuit. A mathematical model was developed for the study, designed to numerically calculate the output signal amplitude depending on the number of coil turns. The model is based on the principle of reciprocity and takes into account the magnetic field of flat solenoid, the distribution of the magnetic field of a permanent magnet, and the magnetostrictive properties of the waveguide material. The model was verified by comparing the data from the computational experiment with the results of field tests on an experimental model with a waveguide made of iron-nickel alloy and a coil on a printed circuit board with 25 turns. A high degree of correspondence between the simulation results and the experimental data was established, confirming the adequacy of model and its applicability for engineering calculations. The results of computational experiments and experimental verification showed that the amplitude value of the signal of a flat spiral solenoid is 75-80% of the signal of a cylindrical solenoid with a similar number of turns, but this difference can be compensated for by technological methods. The results obtained can be used in the design of reproduction channels for magnetostrictive displacement transducers in robotics and industrial automation.

title: Development of a Client-Server Module for Processing Data from Various Transmission Protocols to Demonstrate Work in VR
authors: Ramil Nezhmetdinov, Rostislav Chumak, Damir Bautdinov
abstract: The article discusses the issue of implementing digital production models using real data. A comparative analysis of data transmission protocols and development environments was carried out, an algorithm for the operation of a client-server data processing module from various protocols was proposed, and the module was implemented and tested based on the algorithm. The developed module was implemented programmatically as an additional solution in the ARacle program. The results of the work can serve as a basis for further research in the field of creating digital counterparts of production processes, developing augmented reality systems for industry and improving methods of remote training of personnel at enterprises, and can also be used to create systems for monitoring and diagnosing equipment in real time.

title: Decomposition of Critical Information Infrastructure Objects for Creating a Digital Twin in Information Security Threat Modeling
authors: Evgenii Mityakov, Igor Saenko, Igor Kotenko
abstract: Modern Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) facilities represent complex cyber-physical systems that are vulnerable to targeted cyberattacks capable of causing cascading failures. Traditional information security threat modeling approaches, based on component-oriented analysis, prove insufficient for risk assessment in such systems due to their failure to account for the interconnections between heterogeneous aspects of their operation. This article proposes an approach to creating a Digital Twin (DT) for information security threat modeling, based on the preliminary decomposition of the CII object. The decomposition is performed across five facets: technical, procedural, organizational, functional, and sectoral, with subsequent detailing of each facet in the domains of states and functions. The resulting structured description serves as the basis for forming a logical DT model, whose components unambiguously correspond to the identified aspects of the CII. The main results of the work include: a formalized approach to the decomposition of CII objects, a logical DT model for threat analysis, a definition of the DT's functions within the modeling process, and a description of the DT's integration into threat modeling stages. It is demonstrated that the proposed approach enables the simulation of attacks, consequence forecasting, defense adaptation, and verification of the effectiveness of protective measures in an isolated environment, thereby minimizing the impact on the actual infrastructure.

title: Improving the Use of Sedative Medications in Dental Treatment of Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders Based on AI Technologies
authors: Akhmadkhon Bobokhonov, Jasur Alimdjanovich Rizaev, Shahzod Shavkatovich Xudoykulov
abstract: Dental procedures are challenging for patients with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) due to behavioral disorders, high levels of fear, and sensory sensitivity. In order to ensure safe and effective treatment, various sedative medications are used in dentistry. This study investigated the potential of using artificial intelligence technologies to improve the use of appropriate sedative medications for effective dental procedures in children with ASD. Based on artificial intelligence technologies, Random Forest (RF), XGBoost, and Support Vector Machine (SVM) multi-class classifiers were used to select sedative medications for ASD patients and their accuracies were compared. The study examined the sedation classes of nitrous oxide inhalation, intranasal sedation, intravenous (IV) sedation, and general anesthesia. The models were trained and evaluated based on clinical parameters such as patient age, body weight, ASD level, cooperation level, anxiety index, and dental procedure complexity. A local clinical dataset was developed using anonymized patient records that included these features. Before entering the dataset into the model, pre-processing steps such as filling in missing values, coding categorical columns, and balancing class imbalance were performed. According to the results, the XGBoost model demonstrated the highest overall performance with Accuracy = 96.8%, F1-score = 96.4%. This demonstrated the model’s high predictive ability in data with complex and unbalanced class distribution. The proposed AI approach can optimize the clinical decision-making process by allowing the safe and effective organization of dental treatment in ASD patients, allowing the selection of the type of sedation appropriate to the individual characteristics of the patient.

title: Attention-Based Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Arm Coordination at the Edge Nodes in Industrial Internet of Things
authors: Zhengyuan Li, Wencheng Zou, Jian Guo, Zhengrong Xiang
abstract: Robotic arms serve as critical actuation edge nodes in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)-enabled intelligent manufacturing systems. In distributed industrial cyber-physical architectures, multiple robotic manipulators are required to perform autonomous and cooperative motion planning under obstacle-rich and dynamically coupled environments. However, existing multi-agent deep reinforcement learning approaches often exhibit limited scalability, redundant observation processing, inefficient experience utilization, and unstable convergence when deployed in high-dimensional cooperative scenarios. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an Attention-based Prioritized Trajectory Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (ATP-MADDPG) framework tailored for edge-coordinated multi-arm systems in IIoT environments. The proposed framework incorporates an adaptive attention mechanism to selectively emphasize critical interaction features while suppressing redundant sensory information, thereby enhancing decision efficiency at distributed edge nodes. A prioritized sequence experience replay (PSER) strategy is further introduced to improve the utilization of cooperative trajectory data and accelerate policy evolution. In addition, curriculum learning is employed to enable progressive training and scalable policy refinement for complex multi-arm tasks. Extensive simulations demonstrate that, compared with conventional MADDPG and representative baselines, the proposed ATP-MADDPG achieves higher task success rates, improved cumulative rewards, faster convergence, and enhanced policy stability. These results validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework for distributed cooperative motion planning in IIoT-oriented robotic systems.

title: Through-Wall Human Presence Detection Using Chip-Scale Ultra-Wideband Radar and Edge-Deployed VGG16: A Feasibility Study for Rescue Applications
authors: Juan A. H. Herrera Ollachica, Diego A. Herrera Ollachica, Hiroki Imamura
abstract: After an earthquake, numerous buildings are destroyed, and the frequency of structural collapses increases significantly in regions lacking comprehensive geophysical surveys. Furthermore, the number of deaths increases dramatically in countries without disaster prevention strategies and effective search-and-rescue operations. Consequently, urban search-and-rescue (USAR) teams encounter significant challenges in promptly locating survivors buried beneath substantial rubble. This research presents a feasibility study for detecting human presence by analyzing micro-respiratory motion behind structural obstacles, using a system designed for edge deployment compatible with compact platforms. Initially, millimetric chest movements of survivors were measured using a chip-scale Ultra-Wideband Impulse Radar (IR-UWB radar). Data acquisition over three respiratory cycles was performed by an algorithm proposed in this study. Clutter suppression was accomplished through a moving average filter. A thresholding technique was then applied to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), thereby generating clearer images suitable for training a deep neural network. Following this, a dataset comprising 680 images was created, with 340 images representing scenarios with human presence and 340 without human presence.Aconvolutional neural network (CNN) based on VGG16 was trained via transfer learning to identify human presence behind 28 cm drywall walls. Results indicated that the CNN achieved a validation accuracy of 93.3% [86.67%, 98.33%] with 100% precision, outperforming conventional signal-processing baselines by over 13 percentage points. With an inference time of 23.91 ms on an embedded edge device, this research demonstrated the potential effectiveness of IR-UWB radar as a crucial technology for USAR teams in locating survivors following disasters.

title: MyoDEA: A Self-Sensing Dielectric Elastomer Actuator for Real-Time Muscle Stiffness Monitoring
authors: Seoyeon Ham, Liujun Xu, Siyi Xu
abstract: Monitoring muscle stiffness in real-time during dynamic muscle activity is critical for rehabilitation, athletic training, and human-robot interaction. Existing technologies often require bulky hardware and complex multi-component systems, limiting reliable stiffness measurement in daily activities. In this work, we introduce MyoDEA, a centimeter-scale and 770mg device based on a novel self-sensing dielectric elastomer actuator (DEA) for time-varying muscle stiffness detection. This DEA generates a 2.5 times higher blocked force than previously reported power-dense DEAs at operating frequencies below 10 Hz, while maintaining comparable bandwidth and energy output at resonance. A new self-sensing method is also developed to enable integrated actuation and sensing within a single DEA. The MyoDEA achieves continuous muscle stiffness tracking at 10 Hz over a range of 30 kPa to 140 kPa. Human-subject experiments with MyoDEA worn on the forearm over the flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS) muscle belly demonstrate reliable continuous stiffness tracking during isometric gripping across 0%-80% maximum voluntary contraction (%MVC).

title: Inertial Crosswalk-Crossing Detection for Visually Impaired Navigation Using Deep Learning
authors: F. Serhan Daniş, Valérie Renaudin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The advancement in portable and lightweight sensor technology made the inertial data from daily activities widely accessible, contributing to the interest of tracking these movements or detecting characteristic patterns in these movements. This study focuses on detecting the crosswalk-crossing behavior of visually impaired individuals, which are observed to differ from those of normal sighted people. We hypothesize that a tactile search of the crosswalk endpoint using a cane, a hesitation at the endpoints, or a particular steady walking while crossing are reflected as a unique series of readings in the inertial data. Crosswalk-crossings generate rare and relatively long-duration patterns that regular walking actions, making it challenging to determine which features of the sensory data to focus on. We propose a specially tuned learning framework for detecting crosswalk-crossings using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks, which are known to be effective at data analysis involving temporal hierarchies. Considering also the use cases of the detected crosswalk-crossings, such as correction in the pedestrian positioning systems, we emphasize a precision-focused training with weighted F-scores and a related loss function that minimize false detections. Our findings show that our approach successfully detects real-world crosswalk-crossings with high precision and acceptable recall values. We also show that the models are capable of performing similarly on the data from different devices they are not trained with.

title: Investigation of Charging Mechanisms in PEO With SPIS Simulation Tool
authors: J. Forest, B. Tezenas du Moncel, B. Jeanty-Ruard, A. Trouche, A. Hilgers, D. Cooke
abstract: Strong negative surface charging events, down to several kilovolt negative, have been observed on past missions in polar Earth orbit (PEO), like Swedish/German spacecraft FREJA or U.S. DMSP missions. Through a set of surface charging simulations performed with the Spacecraft Plasma Interaction Software (SPIS) software, this work attempts to explore parameters, related to space plasma conditions and spacecraft characteristics, and especially, the secondary electron yield of coating materials that may favor such events. Results are compared with previous works on this topic. Also, possible consequences on future commercial satellites in PEO, like constellations, are briefly discussed.

title: Deep Radar Perception Network with RTK Supervision for Slow-Moving UAV Detection
authors: Ziyi Jiang, Feng Ke, Yikui Zhai, Jianhong Zhou, Pasquale Coscia, Angelo Genovese, Xiu Yin Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Reliable detection of slow-moving unmanned aerial vehicles under low signal-to-noise ratio conditions remains a core challenge for next-generation intelligent sensing systems. We propose AirSentinel, a novel deep radar perception network to leverage frame-synchronized real-time kinematic-derived geocoordinates for supervised learning, bridging the gap between image-based and radar-based methods. To address the challenge of weak feature discrimination and energy leakage in radar data, we propose a local search and guard band mechanism that identifies Doppler peaks across coherent processing intervals. We further propose an information weight calibration adapter and a global temporal modeling head to facilitate feature alignment across views and capture long-range temporal dependencies, respectively. To mitigate data scarcity, a object transformation and generation scheme is introduced, significantly boosting data diversity and annotation efficiency. Extensive experiments on the newly curated radarUAV-24K dataset demonstrate that AirSentinel outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and real-time performance. This work establishes a robust paradigm for radar-based object detection, with implications for autonomous monitoring and urban airspace security.

title: Salient Object Detection via Cross-Model Interaction and Wavelet-guided Edge Refinement
authors: Pingping Miao, Xiaohui Lin, Mingzhu Xu, Ailin Liu, Zaixiang Wu, Meng Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Salient Object Detection (SOD) aims to locate salient regions in complex visual scenes and segment the most salient objects, playing a crucial role in visual measurement systems. However, it has long faced challenges such as irregular topological structures of salient objects and inaccurate boundary prediction caused by cluttered background interference. Existing methods are limited by the inherent constraints of single-model architectures or suffer from inefficient cross-model fusion during encoding. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel Cross-Model Interaction and Wavelet-guided Edge Refinement Network (CMIWERNet). Specifically, we design a Cross-Model Interaction Module (CMIM) that achieves deep feature collaboration and information exchange during the encoding phase, allowing heterogeneous features to effectively capture both local and global contexts. This enables our model to comprehend the complete topology of salient objects. Meanwhile, a Wavelet-guided Edge Refinement Module (WERM) is introduced to extract edge cues through spatial-frequency analysis and progressively refine the decoding process, thereby suppressing interference from cluttered backgrounds and achieving accurate edge prediction. Experimental evaluations indicate that our method (Ours-V) achieves eighteen best performances and six top-3 performances (excluding Ours-R) among all the twenty-four metrics in six datasets, surpassing existing solutions and offering a more excellent SOD solution for visual measurement applications. The source code will be available at: https://github.com/miaopingping/CMIWERNet.

title: Learning of a Spiking Neural Network with Feedback from the External Environment on Bio Realistic Patterns
authors: Alexander Toschev, Yulia Mikhailova
abstract: This paper presents an approach to training a spiking neural network based on the GRAS (GPU Reflex Arc Simulator) neural simulator, using spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) and data from pressure sensors embedded in wearable insoles. We develop an algorithm that converts sensor time series into spike frequency for the input layer. Prior to encoding, signals are normalized and smoothed to reduce noise and improve spike coding. The resulting network configuration is compared with a reference configuration developed in the Laboratory of Neuromorphic Computing and Neurosimulations, and its effectiveness is assessed using several metrics. In addition, we simulate a walking pattern generated by a central pattern generator, using self-learning STDP driven by pressure-sensor input frequency coding.

title: Context-Driven Automation of Cyber Incident Response in SOC
authors: Maria V. Yakovleva, Aleksandr Y. Glazkov
abstract: In this paper, the authors conduct an extensive study of the current information security incident response algorithm. The paper examines the effectiveness of standard cyber incident response methods in security operations centers (SOCs). Modern SOAR platforms serve as a tool for responding to information security incidents. However, many scenarios are not considered due to the wide range of threats, or are difficult to adapt to multi-stage attacks. This results in a lack of flexibility in resolving such problems, requiring the selection of the right combination of pre-prepared solutions within a short timeframe. This study focuses on the development of a new approach called context-driven automated response. This approach formalizes the use of an artificial intelligence-based system for dynamically adapting response scenarios. An assessment is made of the positive effects and potential technological risks of implementing artificial intelligence into existing cyber defense center tools.

title: Binary Classification of Single-Lead Electrocardiogram Using Machine and Deep Learning Methods
authors: Mariya R. Kiladze, Pavel A. Lyakhov, Ruslan I. Abdulkadirov
abstract: This study presents a comparative analysis of machine and deep learning methods for binary classification of single-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) signals. Five cardiac data classification models were evaluated: decision tree and random forest classification, a long short-term memory (LSTM) network with pre-extraction of random forest and decision tree data, and the proposed hybrid model with parallel feature extraction on a stream of electrocardiogram signals. Experiments were conducted on the publicly available PhysioNet Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2017 dataset labeled as "Normal" or "Atrial Fibrillation." Simulation results showed that the hybrid model with parallel feature extraction is inferior in classification accuracy to the model with pre-extraction of morphological features using the random forest method. These results indicate that machine learning methods, in particular random forest, offer a robust and efficient solution for automatic ECG classification, balancing high performance with low computational cost. This makes them suitable for integration into wearable devices and large-scale screening applications.

title: The Impact of Normalization on Regression-Based Crop Yield Prediction: Accuracy and Efficiency Analysis
authors: Dilmurod Khasanov, Barno Daminova, Maruf Tojiyev
abstract: Normalization is a fundamental step in machine learning data preprocessing, often greatly influencing model performance. This research focuses on the effects of different normalization methods when applied to regression problems by considering both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency. As case studies, this study uses two publicly available datasets of crop yield prediction challenges provided through Kaggle. A total of four widely applied regression algorithms are trained and tested in two experimental settings: with and without data normalization. Their performance was measured using common metrics for the assessment of regression models (RMSE, MAE, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>). To account for computational efficiency, indicators such as training time, memory used, and convergence behavior were considered. The experimental results indicated that for gradient-based algorithms, namely Linear Regression and MLP, normalization mostly played a critical role, enhancing both the accuracy and convergence speed when working with datasets that had heterogeneous feature scales. On the other hand, the effect on tree-based models (RF and GBRT) was insignificant due to their intrinsic scale invariance. Secondly, this study highlights that poor preprocessing choices may lead to a longer training or unstable learning dynamics in neural systems. These findings emphasize the importance of aligning normalization strategies with the nature of the dataset and the algorithm chosen. The results provide practical guidelines for optimizing regression chains of agricultural yield prediction and will generalize to other data-driven decision-making problems in science and industry.

title: Call for Nominations 2026 IEEE Chenming Hu Early Career Award Announcement
authors: N/A

title: Instantaneous Complex Phase and Frequency: Conceptual Clarification and Equivalence between Formulations
authors: César García-Veloso, Mario Paolone, Federico Milano
abstract: This letter seeks to clarify the different existing definitions of both instantaneous complex phase and frequency as well as their equivalence under standard modeling assumptions considered for transmission systems, i.e. balanced positive sequence operation, sole presence of electro-mechanical transient dynamics and absence of harmonics and interharmonics. To achieve this, the two fundamental definitions, i.e., those based on either the use of (i) analytic signals or (ii) space vectors, together with the premises used for their formulation, are presented and their relationship shown. Lastly, a unified notation and terminology to avoid confusion is proposed.

title: CoMAEA: A Collision-Avoiding Multi-Agent Evolutionary Algorithm for Coverage Path Planning_supp4-3665409.mp4
authors: Ting Huang
abstract: Efficient and safe path planning for multiple autonomous agents, such as unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), is essential in many real-world applications. The objective is to coordinate these agents to cover a target area with minimal cost. However, most existing multi-agent coverage path planning (MACPP) methods rely on purely spatial planning and neglect spatio-temporal interactions among agents, which often result in unsafe paths and a high risk of inter-agent collisions during execution. To address this challenge, we propose a new problem formulation, termed collision-aware MACPP (CaMACPP), which incorporates both spatial coverage efficiency and collision avoidance. To solve this problem, we introduce an efficient multi-agent evolutionary algorithm to minimize costs while avoiding collisions. The algorithm first introduces a trapezoidal decomposition strategy to partition complex environments into convex subareas. Then, a cooperative evolutionary algorithm is designed to optimize the inter-area visiting orders for multiple agents and the intra-area coverage modes assigned to each agent. Next, a spatio-temporal collision avoidance mechanism is incorporated to detect and resolve conflicts between agent paths. The proposed algorithm is extensively evaluated against the state-of-the-art MACPP methods to demonstrate its effectiveness in generating collision-free paths with minimal total cost. The results show that it achieves zero collisions in all tested scenarios, reduces the average path length by 15.9%, and decreases coverage overlap by 58.1%.

title: Near-Optimal MDS Codes with Configurable Sub-Packetization_supp1-3687086.pdf
authors: Guodong Li
abstract: Minimum storage regenerating (MSR) codes are a special class of maximum distance separable (MDS) codes that have the optimal repair bandwidth but require an exponentially large sub-packetization. Recent work introduced near-optimal MDS codes that slightly sacrifice repair-bandwidth optimality in exchange for drastically reduced sub-packetization. This paper provides a new construction of near-optimal MDS codes for all parameters (n, k, d) (where repair degree d ∈ {k+1, . . . , n−1}) that offers configurable small sub-packetization ℓ (potentially a constant independent of n) while ensuring the repair-by-transfer and constant repair properties.

title: Power Modules Lifetime Estimation Under Compound Tests
authors: Merouane Ouhab, Nicolas Degrenne, Shin Uegaki
abstract: Reliability assessment of IGBT power modules increasingly requires test conditions that reflect field operation, where the semiconductor devices are subjected to simultaneous fast junction temperature fluctuations and slow baseplate or heatsink temperature variations. Conventional power cycling tests consider these thermal loads separately and therefore fail to capture potential interaction effects that can critically shorten the lifetime. This work investigates experimentally the superimposition effect of active High Frequency (HF) and passive Low Frequency (LF) thermal cycles using dedicated compound tests, provides an analysis of the physical mechanism responsible for the observed lifetime reduction. Active cycles are generated through high-frequency DC current injection, producing junction temperature swings in the range of 30°C-70°C with cycle times of 150ms-18s, while passive cycles are imposed through controlled heatsink temperature variations of 85°C-145°C with time periods from 12min to 1h17min. Failure analysis identifies wire bonds lift off as the dominant degradation mechanism under these compound conditions. Application of conventional Rainflow counting algorithm and Miner's rule overestimate significantly the measured lifetime by a factor exceeding seven, revealing a degradation acceleration due to high- and low-frequency cycles interaction. To explain this effect, an analytical model is developed and qualitatively validated by Finite Element (FE) simulations. The model captures alternating ratcheting phenomenon as a responsible of the observed lifetime reduction, and despite the long and detailed temperature profiles of compound tests, it achieves highly shorter computation times compared to FE approach. The proposed methodology provides an efficient tool to account for superimposed thermal cycles interactions and enhances reliability assessment for power electronic devices.

title: Power-Superimposed SC-FDE for Underwater Integrated Sensing and Communication over Doubly Selective Channels
authors: Yifei Wang, Yutaka Jitsumatsu
abstract: Integrated systems for underwater detection and communication (ISUDC) are expected to play a key role in the Internet of Underwater Things (IoUT). A fundamental challenge in such systems is the trade-off between communication reliability and sensing accuracy. This letter proposes a power-superimposed single-carrier frequency-domain equalization (PS-SC-FDE) framework that explicitly controls this trade-off via a power allocation factor (PAF). Within the proposed framework, an interference cancellation (IC) receiver is employed to mitigate the interference caused by waveform superposition, while a fractional Fourier transform (FrFT)-based processing method is adopted to generate the range–Doppler map for sensing. Simulation results show that the IC receiver provides approximately 0.5–2 dB gain for ρ ∈ [0.5, 0.8], while accurate range–Doppler estimation is maintained in the presence of superimposed interference when ρ ≤ 0.8.

title: Multivariate Sensitivity Analysis of Electric Machine Efficiency Maps and Profiles Under Design Uncertainty
authors: Aylar Partovizadeh, Sebastian Schöps, Dimitrios Loukrezis
abstract: This work introduces the use of multivariate global sensitivity analysis for assessing the impact of uncertain electric machine design parameters on efficiency maps and profiles. Contrary to the common approach of applying variance-based (Sobol') sensitivity analysis elementwise, multivariate sensitivity analysis provides a single sensitivity index per parameter, thus allowing for a holistic estimation of parameter importance over the full efficiency map or profile. Its benefits are demonstrated on permanent magnet synchronous machine models of different fidelity. Computations based on Monte Carlo sampling and polynomial chaos expansions are compared in terms of computational cost. The sensitivity analysis results are subsequently used to simplify the models, by fixing non-influential parameters to their nominal values and allowing random variations only for influential parameters. Uncertainty estimates obtained with the full and reduced models confirm the validity of model simplification guided by multivariate sensitivity analysis.

title: A Novel Gradual Inference Approach for Handling Perturbations in Sparse CrowdSensing
authors: En Wang, Zixuan Song, Wenbin Liu, Jing Deng, Bo Yang, Yongjian Yang
abstract: Sparse CrowdSensing has emerged as a promising paradigm for data collection, which utilizes mobile devices to gather partial sensing data and infer the remaining ones. Most existing studies focus on data inference. However, they often overlook the impact of unforeseen circumstances. These perturbations can lead to many issues in the data inference process such as over-correction (adjustments are excessively applied), perturbation diffusion (errors affecting related data), and data bias (overall trends in inferred data deviating from the truth). To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose a novel approach called Gradual Matrix Completion (GMC) for handling perturbations in Sparse CrowdSensing. This method distinguishes itself by inferring adjacent unsensed data and using it as a basis for ongoing optimization, gradually completing the entire data matrix. Through the gradual inference process, GMC mitigates perturbation diffusion by continuously refining feature selection, thus preserving and reconstructing essential features. Moreover, GMC concentrates on both intra-area and inter-area spatio-temporal relationships, progressively enhancing its understanding of local and global dependencies. Evaluation of the GMC approach on four diverse real-world datasets highlights its robust capability to complete perturbed data and cope with noise in Sparse CrowdSensing.

title: Enhancing Positioning Accuracy of Wrist-Worn Instrumentation: A Factor Graph-Based Fusion of GNSS and Inertial Measurements
authors: Jiale Wang, Kunlei Li, Ming Xia, Chuang Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate trajectory measurement and positioning using consumer-grade wrist-worn devices present significant challenges due to the inherent noise, bias, and drift of low-cost MEMS inertial sensors, as well as the uncertainty of GNSS signals in obstructed environments. While pedestrian dead reckoning (PDR) is a common technique, it suffers from cumulative measurement errors, whereas standalone GNSS is susceptible to signal outages. To address these instrumentation limitations, this paper proposes a robust PDR/GNSS integrated measurement method based on factor graph optimization (FGO). Unlike traditional filtering approaches, the proposed framework models the positioning problem as a joint optimization of GNSS observations, PDR-derived motion states, and velocity constraints within a globally consistent graph. Specifically, to mitigate the systematic errors in inertial measurements, a step frequency detection and adaptive step length estimation model is developed, effectively serving as an in-run calibration for the wrist-worn inertial measurement unit (IMU). The FGO framework then optimally estimates the trajectory by minimizing the measurement residuals through a nonlinear least-squares optimization using the Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm. Experimental validation conducted in diverse scenarios demonstrates that the proposed method significantly reduces the measurement uncertainty, achieving average positioning error reductions of 34.1%, 12.6%, and 29.8% in open, GNSS-challenged, and transition environments, respectively, compared to conventional methods. These results confirm the efficacy of the proposed approach in enhancing the measurement accuracy and reliability of wearable instrumentation without reliance on external infrastructure.

title: General Type-2 Hierarchical Fuzzy Model-Agnostic Explanation of Image Classification for XAI_supp1-3686974.pdf
authors: Hak-Keung Lam
abstract: Growing complexity of closed box image classifiers has heightened the demand for explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) to offer transparency and trust for model decisions. Model-agnostic approaches have gained attention, as they provide explanations without accessing the closed box's internals, making them ideal for scenarios requiring data privacy and security. However, existing methods relying on superpixels face four challenges: (1) limited scalability, as superpixel-based features are coupled to specific image and cannot generalize; (2) low comprehensibility, due to the high dimensionality of superpixels and absence of clear semantic meaning; (3) lack of robustness, since uncertainty modeling is absent in explanation methods; (4) poor transferability, as integrating knowledge from human and explanations remains challenging. To alleviate these challenges, general type-2 hierarchical fuzzy model-agnostic explanation of image classification (GT2-HFMAE-I) is proposed to explain decisions of any closed box image classifier. Our innovations and contributions include the proposal of the framework, the algorithm, and the interface of GT2-HFMAE-I. First, the biaxial hierarchical framework is presented to formulate explanations with scalability along the spatial axis with local, domain and universe levels and the semantic axis with different segmentation granularity. Second, the model-agnostic algorithm is developed to identify explanatory features of image with semantic comprehensibility in low-dimensional space by combining superpixels and semantic segmentation, and train a general type-2 fuzzy logic system to approximate the closed box model with robustness to handle uncertainty in explanations. Third, a user interface, with feature salience to highlight image segments as the decision basis and semantic inference to present the decision logic via IF-THEN rules, is designed to deliver explanations to users with transferability. Finally, experiments with explanatory metrics show the competitive explanation quality of our method compared to mainstream model-agnostic methods on multiple datasets.

title: Output Feedback Stabilization of Reaction-Diffusion Equations in $H^{2}$-norm
authors: Hugo Lhachemi, Christophe Prieur
abstract: This paper studies the problem of boundary output feedback stabilization of reaction-diffusion partial differential equations (PDEs) in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-norm. While most of the control strategies reported in the literature embrace the case of the either <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$L^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>- of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H^{1}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-norm, very few address the case of the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-norm. This is achieved in this paper for linear reaction-diffusion PDEs with spatially varying coefficients and with a either Neumann or Robin boundary control (but excludes the case of a Dirichlet boundary control) by leveraging spectral reduction methods. The obtained results are extended to three application cases that all specifically require the study of the system trajectories in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-norm for deploying spectral reduction methods. The first one is concerned with the derivation of a robustness result with respect to small linear unbounded in-domain perturbations that include the case of weighted local Dirichlet and Neumann traces. The second one addresses the local stabilization of a class of generalized Hamilton-Jacobi equations. The last one embraces the case of a saturated Neumann measurement for the local exponential stabilization of the plant with explicit estimation of a subset of the domain of attraction.

title: Formation of a Digital Twin of an Object's Geometry for Electrical Impedance Tomography Tasks
authors: Vitaliy S. Cherednichenko, Mikhail V. Lankin, Igor M. Lankin
abstract: The article discusses a method for forming a digital twin of the geometry of the patient's thorax cross-section based on non-contact measurement data for use in electroimpedance tomography tasks. The study is aimed at obtaining an individualized geometric model of the patient's chest, which ensures a more correct definition of the boundaries of the calculated area during subsequent mathematical modeling. An array of laser time-of-flight rangefinders located around the perimeter of the measuring base is used as the source of the initial data, which allows recording distances to the surface of an object without physical contact. A model of the measuring base and a method for restoring the coordinates of points on the surface of an object have been developed, which ensures the formation of a digital twin of the geometry of the section. Experimental studies have been carried out using measuring bases of round and ellipsoidal shapes, and errors in the reconstruction of geometric parameters have been estimated. It is shown that the proposed method provides high accuracy of forming a digital twin geometry. The results obtained confirm the expediency of using contactless measurements to build digital geometric models and demonstrate the prospects of the proposed approach for use in computing complexes of electroimpedance tomography and other biomedical measuring systems.

title: Improving the Reliability of Monitoring Methods for Railway Automation and Telemechanics Wayside Equipment through the Use of a Hybrid Network Based on GSM and CDMA
authors: Abdulaziz T. Botirov, Natalya V. Yaronova, Janibek F. Kurbanov
abstract: This article discusses methods for improving the maintenance process of railway automation and telemechanics wayside equipment by using a hybrid communication network based on GSM and CDMA standards. The study focuses on solving an important problem related to increasing the reliability of continuous maintenance of wayside equipment on the sections of Uzbekistan Railways. The proposed hybrid communication network helps to eliminate signal interruptions, coverage gaps, and transmission delays during data exchange. By combining the advantages of GSM and CDMA technologies, such as wide coverage, high resistance to interference, and strong data transmission capability, a stable and uninterrupted communication environment is achieved. The article also presents approaches aimed at improving the efficiency and reliability of technical maintenance through the practical use of these hybrid technologies.

title: Architecture of a Social Engineering Security System Using Artificial Intelligence
authors: Andrey Kyuner, Andrey Chechulin
abstract: Currently, there is a widespread adoption of Artificial Intelligence technologies, particularly for solving information security problems. Social engineering remains one of the most pressing threats in the context of cybersecurity and financial fraud. Modern adversaries leverage AI tools to craft specific elements of social engineering attacks, while security developers employ AI to detect these components. Traditional organizational measures and training programs often fail to ensure effective detection due to the inherent psychological vulnerabilities of the victims. Practice demonstrates that an experienced cybersecurity officer is most capable of accurately identifying signs of a social engineering attack. Consequently, the development of intelligent systems should focus on automating the duties of a security specialist. The objective of this paper is to design the architecture of an AI-based software complex capable of learning, much like a security specialist, to recognize suspicious messages by analyzing their elements and mapping them against known social engineering techniques. The article outlines the advantages and disadvantages of existing scientific approaches to social engineering protection. This paper provides an example of applying the architecture to protect against social engineering attacks in instant messengers. The article also describes previous and the latest inventions in cybersecurity sphere related to social engineering countermeasures.

title: Evaluation of Classical and Ensemble Machine Learning Algorithms in KNN-Based Missing Data Imputation
authors: Fayzullo Nazarov, Abror Akhatov, Sanjar Kenjaev
abstract: This study examined methods for analyzing data with complex structures, extreme values, and NaN values using machine learning models. The techniques of removing NaN values and using KNN imputation to fill in the dataset were compared. After the dataset was processed in different ways, it was evaluated using both conventional machine learning techniques and the ensemble learning-based XGB model. The results showed that applying KNN imputation to the data improved the accuracy of almost all models. The XGB ensemble learning model obtained 85% accuracy after KNN imputation and 82% accuracy after NaN values were eliminated. The findings demonstrate that using imputation techniques to deal with missing data significantly improves the generalizability of models.

title: Agentic AI for UAV Trajectory Optimization and Fair Computation Offloading in Complex Low-Altitude Environments
authors: Qiang Liu, Zhisen Zhang, Da Chen, Ronghui Zhang, Yongkang Gong, Arumugam Nallanathan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: With the emergence of the low-altitude economy, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have become pivotal components in mobile edge computing (MEC) architectures, leveraging their mobility and line-of-sight (LoS) advantages for computation offloading. However, autonomous operation in complex environments requires addressing two critical challenges: generating collision-free trajectories amidst dense obstacles while ensuring fair resource allocation among heterogeneous users with time-varying demands. This paper presents a unified agentic artificial intelligence (AI) framework that jointly optimizes obstacle avoidance, trajectory planning, and fairness-aware scheduling. By treating the UAV as an autonomous agent, the framework endows the system with goal-driven autonomy through five collaborative modules, namely perception, decision, execution, self-evaluation, and memory, enabling continuous closed-loop operation rather than relying on manually designed rules. Deep reinforcement learning (DRL), specifically the Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm, serves as the underlying engine. The state representation integrates spatial localization, obstacle proximity, and service history, while the reward function balances fairness, user energy consumption, and collision avoidance. Comparative evaluations against Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) demonstrate 5%–10% improvements in fairness while maintaining collision-free operation. Results validate the effectiveness of the proposed agentic AI framework for autonomous UAV operations in complex low-altitude environments.

title: A Distributed Scheduling Algorithm for TDMA in Diffusion-Based Molecular Communications: Concept and Practical Consideration
authors: Michael Gattringer, Stefan Angerbauer, Andreas Springer, Werner Haselmayr
publish: IEEE Transactions on Molecular, Biological, and Multi-Scale Communications
abstract: This paper proposes a distributed scheduling algorithm for time-division multiple access in diffusion-based molecular communication systems. We consider multiple transmitters sending messages to a single receiver. The proposed scheduling algorithm is executed by each transmitter and prevents interuser interference in its final operating state. Moreover, it requires no synchronization among the transmitters and allows non-ideal time measurement and varying package lengths. The efficiency of the proposed algorithm is compared with an ideal and centralized scheduling algorithm as an upper benchmark and is validated with a particle-based simulation.

title: DQN-based Fair Resource Unit Allocation for Multi-AP Coordinated OFDMA Wi-Fi Networks
authors: Mahboubeh Irannezhad Parizi, Mostafa Rahmani Ghourtani, David Nunez, Francesc Wilhelmi, Boris Bellalta, Frank Scahill, Kanapathippillai Cumanan
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiple Access (OFDMA) was introduced in IEEE 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) with the key goal of facilitating concurrent transmissions of multiple stations (STAs) with partitioning the available bandwidth into resource units (RUs). However, allocating RUs efficiently is pivotal to maximizing the performance of the Wi-Fi networks. Coordinated OFDMA (C-OFDMA) is now being proposed as an option for future Wi-Fi networks (initially targeting the 802.11bn, i.e., Wi-Fi 8) to enhance the network’s latency and improve reliability, especially in dense areas with multi-RU allocation. However, incorporating advanced techniques such as C-OFDMA with multi-RU allocation, while beneficial, significantly increases system complexity. To ensure both improved network throughput and equitable resource distribution, RU allocation must be dynamically adapted to the specific requirements of each STA and the overall network. This work first underscores the significance of variable RU allocation in relation to heterogeneous long-term channel conditions among STAs. Subsequently, a max-min throughput fairness-based RU allocation scheme is proposed, aiming to allocate RUs in a way that maximize the STAs with minimum throughput by taking into account the STAs’ heterogeneous long-term channel conditions under the assumption of fully buffered traffic. Given the inherent complexity of RU allocation and the NP-hard nature of the considered problem, an offline deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based approach is considered to facilitate variable RU-sized allocation according to STAs’ heterogeneous long-term channel conditions. Simulation results first confirm the high computational complexity of the problem, and then demonstrate that the proposed DRL-based algorithm effectively simplifies and enables its practical deployment. Furthermore, the results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms conventional equal-sized and random RU allocation methods in terms of max-min fairness and Jain’s fairness index.

title: Integrated Energy-Efficient Scheduling of Distributed Flow Shop and Distribution Using a Q-Learning-Based Multi-Objective Cooperation Evolutionary Algorithm_supp1-3684022.docx
authors: Zhengpei Zhang
abstract: Production and distribution are indispensable in a supply chain. Practically, finding an overall optimization of the two processes has become an urgent problem. This article addresses an integrated energy-efficient scheduling problem of distributed flow shop and distribution in the context of economic globalization and environment sustainability. First, a mixed integer programing model is formulized to achieve minimal maximum completion time, minimal total energy consumption and minimal maximum workload of factories. Second, a Q-learning-based multi-objective cooperation evolutionary algorithm with problem-specific knowledge is specially developed. A cooperation strategy is devised to search a solution space using three populations. Three heuristic rules are proposed to initialize the populations. A knowledge-based local search is devised to refine better individuals. A Q-learning-based approach is designed to adaptively choose premium search strategies at each iteration. Finally, the designed method is compared with three meta-heuristics in prior research and a mathematical programming solver CPLEX. The comparison experiments are conducted by using a group of benchmark instances and a real-world case. The outcomes confirm outstanding capacities of the developed model and algorithm for dealing with the considered problems.
