title: Consensus-Based Privacy-Preserving Energy Management Strategies Based on Output Mask Approaches
authors: Wenjing An, Derui Ding, Qinyuan Liu, Xingzhen Bai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The distributed energy management (DEM) problem of smart grids is devoted to achieving optimal energy dispatch and allocation to ensure social welfare maximization. It can be modelled as a distributed optimization problem with physical constraints, whose solution depends on data sharing between smart devices. The exchange of information creates a potential risk for eavesdroppers to intercept and access private data. To avoid privacy leakages, a privacy-preserving optimization strategy via output masks in a consensus framework is proposed to realize social welfare maximization of energy management, where power demand relies on the parameters of the demand sides. Theoretical analysis discloses that eavesdroppers are unable to accurately infer private information of the generators/demand loads. Furthermore, both the convergence and optimality of the proposed strategy are discussed by means of the matrix perturbation theory and the famous KKT optimality condition. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed strategy for solving the DEM problem is confirmed by the simulation experiments.

title: Computed torque for restricted Lagrange-Euler systems via DAEs and LMIs
authors: Julián León, David Vázquez, Miguel Bernal
abstract: An extension of the well-known computed-torque technique for restricted Lagrange-Euler systems is presented in this work. It is shown that adding reaction forces to the feedforward term as well as designing the feedback term via linear matrix inequalities allows computed torque to deal with trajectory tracking problems in closed kinematic chains. Implementation issues via index-1 differential algebraic equations and recently appeared toolboxes are discussed. A fully reproducible case study is included to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposal as well as a real-time implementation which point out that non-restricted plants are also amenable to the novel technique.

title: SRC-TDNN: Split Residual Collaborative Time Delay Neural Network for Short-Speech Speaker Verification
authors: Fei Deng, Lihong Deng, Peifan Jiang
abstract: Systems based on Time Delay Neural Networks (TDNNs) have achieved remarkable success in speaker verification due to their favorable balance between accuracy and computational cost. However, under short-utterance conditions, two limitations arise: limited information hinders the extraction of discriminative speaker features, and the inherent one-dimensional (1D) convolutions lack translation equivariance to frequency shifts, restricting robustness and feature utilization. To address these issues, we propose SRC-TDNN (Split Residual Collaborative TDNN), a lightweight architecture that integrates a two-dimensional (2D) convolutional component with a TDNN component. The 2D convolutional component employs parallel split residual convolutional (SplitResConv) blocks to capture multi-scale time–frequency features, followed by a Fourier Fusion Module (FFM) to enhance feature discriminability. By sharing weights along the frequency axis, this component achieves translation equivariance, alleviating the impact of frequency shifts in short utterances. The TDNN component further aggregates the fused features along the temporal axis while inheriting the translation equivariance from the 2D component. It also adopts parallel 1D SplitResConv blocks to capture both short-term phonetic details and long-term speaker characteristics across multiple temporal scales. A multi-branch architecture is employed throughout, enabling both network-level ( macro) and block-level (micro) multi-scale modeling to better exploit limited information. Evaluations on four test sets with varying utterance lengths demonstrate that the proposed method achieves the best overall performance, particularly under short-utterance conditions. Compared with ECAPA-TDNN, SRC-TDNN reduces the number of parameters by only 0.4 M while achieving at least a 30% relative improvement.

title: Sub-THz Signal Distribution Based on Additively Manufactured Dielectric Image Lines
authors: Leonhard Hahn, Lukas Bürk, Tobias Bader, David Panusch, Christian Carlowitz, Gerald Gold, Martin Vossiek
abstract: The realization of low-loss multi-channel power splitters at sub-THz frequencies is highly challenging, as conventional transmission-line-based approaches suffer from severe performance limitations, low scalability, or high fabrication costs. To illustrate an attractive solution to this problem, this work presents a broadband sub-THz signal distribution network based on additively manufactured dielectric image lines (DIL), enabling symmetric 1x6 power splitting across the 140–220 GHz range. For the first time, additive manufacturing (AM) is used to integrate various dielectric components like transmission lines, 1x2 and 1x4 splitters, a directional coupler with configurable power division ratio, and a dielectric termination into a large-scale, monolithic network. The concept is manufactured using up to four different AM techniques to investigate the impact of fabrication characteristics on network performance. Manufacturing artifacts are analyzed and modeled, revealing the impact of adhesion, surface quality, and process complexity onto key performance metrics like efficiency, bandwidth, port symmetry, and reproducibility.

title: Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Subcarrier and Power Allocation Among Joint Communication and Radar System
authors: Fengde Jia, Siyu Cao, Hengrong Cui, Junhui Qian
abstract: Despite the advantages of the joint communication and radar (JCR) system, such as cost-effectiveness and high resource utilization, it also faces issues like resource conflicts and poor environmental adaptability. To address the above issues, this paper proposes a subcarrier and power allocation scheme based on multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MD-SAPA). Subcarrier and power allocation in a multi-base station downlink JCR system are treated as optimization variables, and an optimization strategy is designed to maximize the weighted sum of communication throughput and radar mutual information. A multi-agent double deep Q-Network (MADDQN) algorithm is adopted to dynamically select the optimal subcarrier based on fluctuating traffic demands and subchannel correlation information. Meanwhile, a multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG) algorithm is introduced, which efficiently learns the optimal power allocation strategy in a continuous action space. By integrating these two algorithms, the system can achieve intelligent scheduling of spectral and power resources, ensuring both communication and radar performance while improving overall resource utilization efficiency. Simulation results demonstrate that, under scenarios with identical data demands, the proposed algorithm outperforms other baseline algorithms regarding actual throughput and overall system performance.

title: Fuzzy Fractional-Order Adaptive Control for Mobility-as-a-Service Orchestration: A Cybernetic Approach *Achieving Complete Decision Coverage Through 2,187-Rule Architecture
authors: Ahmed Elsayed, Ghassan Malkawi, Firuz Kamalov
abstract: Mobility-as-a-Service (MaaS) orchestration faces critical challenges in managing the Service Quality Index (SQI), Fleet Utilization Ratio (FUR), and Dynamic Pricing Index (DPI) under uncertain and time-varying urban demand. Existing approaches—including multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) and hybrid learning-optimization methods—lack provable stability guarantees and systematic uncertainty handling, limiting their reliability for safety-critical urban mobility systems. This paper introduces the first control-theoretic framework for MaaS that simultaneously provides fractional-order modeling, complete fuzzy rule coverage, and Lyapunov-guaranteed stability. The proposed Fractional-Order Adaptive Fuzzy Controller (FOAFC) embodies Ashby’s Law of Requisite Variety through a dual mechanism: a proportional component that attenuates large-scale disturbances and provides global stability, and a complete 2,187-rule fuzzy inference system that amplifies fine-grained decision variety near the operating equilibrium—together matching controller complexity to the inherent variety of the MaaS operational envelope. Fractional-order Grünwald-Letnikov dynamics with differentiation order q = 0.99 are employed to explicitly capture the hereditary memory effects of urban traffic flow, including long-range congestion propagation and demand correlation timescales of approximately 30 seconds, which integer-order models structurally fail to represent. Lyapunov stability is rigorously established using the Fractional Barbalat’s Lemma, providing asymptotic convergence guarantees despite parametric uncertainties and nonlinear system dynamics. Simulation results demonstrate 95.35% convergence to the target equilibrium—representing an optimal engineering tradeoff between tracking accuracy and stability, as gains beyond the critical value K2 = 195 cause complete system failure—alongside 95.7% RMSE reduction over classical PID and triple dominance over pure adaptive control across RMSE, settling time (53.4 s, 4.3% faster), and control energy (20,402 units, 1.1% lower). Near-perfect operational compliance is achieved: 100% SQI adherence, 99.3% FUR efficiency, and 100% DPI stability. Monte Carlo validation across 30 independent trials under ±20% initial condition variations and ±15% parameter perturbations yields a 100% success rate and coefficient of variation of 3.47%, confirming robust, production-ready performance. This work provides a mathematically rigorous and operationally viable foundation for next-generation urban mobility systems.

title: In-Network Machine Learning for Real-Time Patient Monitoring on IoMT Edge Gateways
authors: Aristide Tanyi-Jong Akem, Huiqi Yvonne Lu, Noa Zilberman
abstract: The Internet of Medical Things is transforming healthcare from reactive, hospital-based care to preventive and continuous remote monitoring. However, current solutions often depend on cloud or mobile platforms for data aggregation and analysis, introducing latency, privacy risks, and financial burden. In-network machine learning offers an opportunity to address these challenges by enabling real-time analytics directly within the network infrastructure. In this work, we present VISTA, an in-network computing framework for health analytics that enables real-time patient monitoring through in-network machine learning within edge gateways. VISTA integrates P4-based data plane modules that asynchronously aggregate heterogeneous sensor data and execute machine learning inference locally within the gateway, eliminating the reliance on cloud offloading. Implemented on a Dell Edge Gateway and evaluated on a 2000-patient dataset for early detection of sepsis and heart failure, VISTA achieves up to 95% detection accuracy, 2 milliseconds average latency, and 90% reduction in communication overhead, compared with cloud-based baselines. These results demonstrate the potential of in-network machine learning for scalable, low-latency, and privacy-preserving remote patient monitoring.

title: Music-Enhanced Brain Connectivity and Memory Performance: Comparative Analysis of Traditional and Digital Learning Mediums Using EEG
authors: Keerthik Dhivya Rajakumar, Jagannath Mohan
abstract: Extensive research has demonstrated that music enhances cognitive functions, such as learning, reasoning, and memory. This study investigated the influence of music on short- and long-term memory during learning through traditional and digital mediums in healthy adult participants. Twenty-seven participants engaged in memory tasks under both learning conditions while electroencephalogram (EEG) data were collected. Functional and effective brain connectivity features in both the time and frequency domains were extracted and analyzed using SHAP-based feature interpretation. Node-level connectivity descriptors and edge-level off-diagonal connectivity representations derived from connectivity features were utilized for brain connectivity pattern classification. A hybrid ensemble learning architecture consisting of two ProtoNet-MLP models and a calibrated SVM as base classifiers, with logistic regression as the meta classifier was employed. Stronger significant theta band connectivity was observed during memory tasks with music particularly under long-term memory conditions, suggesting a potential association between music exposure and memory consolidation. Long-term memory exhibited a significantly broader network involving frontal, temporal, parietal, and occipital regions compared with short-term memory. In addition, differences in connectivity organization were observed between learning modalities, where traditional learning showed relatively synchronized connectivity patterns while digital learning exhibited more complex unsynchronized interaction patterns. The proposed model achieved mean accuracies of 65.17% and 59.5% under subject-independent protocol for digital and traditional learning conditions, whereas 69.06% and 63.5% under within-subject protocol, reflecting inter-subject variability impact on performance. These findings suggest that music may influence memory-related brain connectivity and provide insights into the brain connectivity patterns across different learning mediums.

title: RF-Based Drone Classification Using Multi-Scale Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Van-Bac Nguyen, Anh-Tu Nguyen-Ngoc, Van-Phuc Hoang, Van-Sang Doan
abstract: !Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV or drone) classification is critical for surveillance, enabling the identification and distinction of drones for military, civilian, and other security applications. Although deep learning models trained on radio frequency (RF) signals show promise for drone classification, they often lack robustness in real-world and variable-noise environments. This limitation stems from their reliance on static feature fusion strategies, which cannot adaptively prioritize the most discriminative time-frequency information. To address this limitation, we propose a multi-scale convolutional neural network (MS-CNN) enhanced with a novel attention mechanism. Specifically, our model dynamically learns to weight multi-scale features extracted from Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) representations of RF signals, enabling it to focus on the most relevant signal characteristics under varying noise levels. Trained on a mixed signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) dataset augmented with synthetic additive white gaussian noise (AWGN), the MS-CNN learns inherent robustness without requiring explicit noise-specific tuning. When evaluated on the large-scale DroneRFa dataset, which comprises 24 drone classes and one noise class under controlled AWGN across an SNR range of -20 dB to 10 dB, our model achieves a peak accuracy of 99.04% at 0 dB SNR and consistently outperforms existing benchmark methods.

title: Risk-Assessment and Visual Perception of Autonomous Microrobots using Model-free Reinforcement Learning to Cope with Multi-challenging Environments_supp4-3686841.mp4
authors: Laliphat Manamanchaiyaporn

title: Investigating Temporal Delays in Time-Series Forecasts: Detection and Quantification with the n-Step-Shifting Method
authors: Huseyin Burak Akyol, Chris Preist, Daniel Schien
abstract: Time-series forecasting is fundamental to decision-making across numerous domains; however, systematic temporal delays in predictions remain a largely overlooked or unrecognised phenomenon. This phenomenon occurs when forecasts regress toward recent observations, resulting in predicted series that closely mirror actual data, but trail behind in time. Such temporal misalignment in predictions has been reported in the literature to undermine model evaluation, method comparison and ranking, and the transferability of forecasting studies, especially in contexts with volatile and irregular time-series patterns. Moreover, prior studies have shown that widely used standard evaluation metrics fail to detect such delays, often leading to misleadingly optimistic assessments of predictive accuracy. Therefore, systematic detection and quantification of such delays in predictions is essential before deploying forecasting models in practice to obtain more reliable, robust, and transferable forecasting practices. To this end, in this study, we make three key contributions through conducted experimental studies using real-world electricity and gas consumption datasets: (i) we demonstrate that temporal delays are not necessarily limited to one time step, contrary to what is often assumed in the literature, and may extend to two or more steps depending on the correlation structure of the underlying data and the choice of input features; (ii) we propose a quantitative n-Step-Shifting (n-SS) method that enables the detection of delays of arbitrary length. This method provides a simple but robust mechanism to identify temporal displacement in forecasts; and (iii) we show that, although they do not directly detect temporal displacement, relative error metrics using a persistence model as baseline have the potential to exhibit a degree of resilience against the deceptive effects of such systematic delays and may provide a basis for the quantification of temporal displacement.

title: Federated Multimodal Bi-LSTM for Agentic Handover in 6G Heterogeneous Networks
authors: Wei-Che Chien, Yu Huang, Cheng Dai, Xingang Liu, Kuljeet Kaur, Mubarak Alrashoud
abstract: In 6G Heterogeneous Networks (HetNets), conventional handover mechanisms such as 3GPP Event A3 rely on single-metric thresholds and static parameters. These reactive schemes are often inadequate in dense and highly dynamic deployments, leading to handover failures, pingpong effects, and degraded user experience. This article presents a tutorial overview of an agentic handover framework in which the user equipment (UE) acts as an intelligent agent that predicts its future motion and radio conditions. The framework combines multimodal sequence prediction based on a federated multi-task bidirectional long short-term memory (Bi-LSTM) model with a prediction-driven handover controller. The model jointly forecasts UE trajectory and multi-cell signal indicators while preserving data privacy through federated learning. On top of these predictions, a cosine-similarity-based base station selection strategy and a dynamic Time-to-Trigger (TTT) adaptation scheme are used to reduce unnecessary handovers and ping-pong events. Simulation results in representative 6G HetNet scenarios show that the proposed approach suppresses ping-pong probability to approximately 12–21% (representing a relative reduction of up to 63% compared with classical Event A3/A5 handover strategies), while remaining lightweight enough for edge deployment.

title: Poisson Hail on a Wireless Ground
authors: François Baccelli, Ke Feng, Sergey Foss
abstract: This paper defines a new model which incorporates three key ingredients of a large class of wireless communication systems: (1) spatial interactions through interference, (2) dynamics of the queueing type, with users joining and leaving, and (3) carrier sensing and collision avoidance as used in, e.g., WiFi. In systems using (3), rather than directly accessing the shared resources upon arrival, a customer is considerate and waits to access them until nearby users in service have left. This new model can be seen as a missing piece of a larger puzzle that contains such dynamics as spatial birth-and-death processes, the Poisson-Hail model, and wireless dynamics as key other pieces. It is shown that, under natural assumptions, this model can be represented as a Markov process on the space of counting measures. The main results are then two-fold. The first result is on the shape of the stability region and, more precisely, on the characterization of the critical value of the arrival rate that separates stability from instability. The second result is of a more qualitative or perhaps even ethical nature. There is evidence that for natural values of the system parameters, the implementation of sensing and the delayed access for collision avoidance can stabilize a system that would be unstable if immediate access to the shared resources would be granted. In other words, for these parameters, renouncing greedy access makes sharing sustainable, whereas indulging in greedy access kills the system.

title: Highly Sensitive Triple-Band Terahertz Metasensor for Multi-Disease Detection
authors: K. B. S. Sri Nagini, Bappadittya Roy
abstract: Terahertz (THz) metasurface absorbers (MSAs) have emerged as highly promising devices for biomedical sensing due to their strong near-field confinement and high-quality resonances. In this article, a triple-band hybrid MSA consisting of square ring, circular ring, and modified Jerusalem cross resonators for multi-disease detection with high angular stability (up to 70°) is presented. The proposed design achieved near-perfect absorption (>99%) at 1.37, 3.06, and 3.91THz, with quality factors (QFs) of 23.87, 55.13, and 37.94, respectively. The metasensor provides high sensitivity up to 2.4THz/refractive index unit (RIU) and a figure of merit (FOM) of 61.53, covering a wide refractive index range (1.35–1.5). Owing to the triple-band operation, strong field confinement, and angular stability, the design enables precise detection of multiple biological analytes, including cancer biomarkers, hemoglobin variations, and tumor markers, providing a compact and robust platform for advanced, label-free THz biosensing applications. The integration of three dissimilar resonators within a single compact metasurface enables independent resonance control and multifrequency biosensing using a single device.

title: SABER: Symbolic Regression-based Angle of Arrival and Beam Pattern Estimator
authors: Shih-Kai Chou, Mengran Zhao, Cheng-Nan Hu, Kuang-Chung Chou, Carolina Fortuna, Jernej Hribar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate Angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimation is essential for next-generation wireless communication systems to enable reliable beamforming, high-precision localization, and integrated sensing. Unfortunately, classical high-resolution techniques require multi-element arrays and extensive snapshot collection, while generic Machine Learning (ML) approaches often yield black-box models that lack physical interpretability. To address these limitations, we propose a Symbolic Regression (SR)-based ML framework. Namely, Symbolic Regression-based Angle of Arrival and Beam Pattern Estimator (SABER), a constrained symbolic-regression framework that automatically discovers closed-form beam pattern and AoA models from path loss measurements with interpretability. SABER achieves high accuracy while bridging the gap between opaque ML methods and interpretable physics-driven estimators. First, we validate our approach in a controlled free-space anechoic chamber, showing that both direct inversion of the known cosn beam and a low-order polynomial surrogate achieve sub-0.5 degree Mean Absolute Error (MAE). A purely unconstrained SR method can further reduce the error of the predicted angles, but produces complex formulas that lack physical insight. Then, we implement the same SR-learned inversions in a real-world, Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS)-aided indoor testbed. SABER and unconstrained SR models accurately recover the true AoA with near-zero error. Finally, we benchmark SABER against the Cram´er-Rao Lower Bounds (CRLBs). Our results demonstrate that SABER is an interpretable and accurate alternative to stateof- the-art and black-box ML-based methods for AoA estimation.

title: EMORe: Motion-Robust 5D MRI Reconstruction via Expectation-Maximization-Guided Binning Correction and Outlier Rejection_supp1-3686805.mp4
authors: Syed M. Arshad
abstract: We propose EMORe, an adaptive reconstruction method designed to enhance motion robustness in free-running, free-breathing self-gated 5D cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Traditional self-gating-based motion binning for 5D MRI often results in residual motion artifacts due to inaccuracies in cardiac and respiratory signal extraction and sporadic bulk motion, compromising clinical utility. EMORe addresses these issues by integrating adaptive inter-bin correction and explicit outlier rejection within an expectation-maximization (EM) framework, whereby the E-step and M-step are executed alternately until convergence. In the E-step, probabilistic (soft) bin assignments are refined by correcting misassignment of valid data and rejecting motion-corrupted data to a dedicated outlier bin. In the M-step, the image estimate is improved using the refined soft bin assignments. Validation in a simulated 5D MRXCAT phantom demonstrated EMORe’s superior performance compared to standard compressed sensing reconstruction, showing significant improvements in peak signal-to-noise ratio, structural similarity index, edge sharpness, and bin assignment accuracy across varying levels of simulated bulk motion. In vivo validation in 13 volunteers further confirmed EMORe’s robustness, significantly enhancing blood-myocardium edge sharpness and reducing motion artifacts compared to compressed sensing, particularly in scenarios with controlled coughing-induced motion. Although EMORe incurs a modest increase in computational complexity, its adaptability and robust handling of bulk motion artifacts improves image quality, supporting its potential for improved clinical applicability and diagnostic confidence of 5D cardiac MRI.

title: Artificial Intelligence in the Analysis of Emotional Coloring of Text Communications
authors: Alexey Plotnikov, Irina Tolstova, Lyudmila Korobova
abstract: With the digital transformation of manufacturing, a significant portion of communications in Industry 4.0 is conducted in text form: through corporate information systems, technical documentation, support chats, customer feedback, and so on. This increases the need for automated systems for analyzing the emotional content of text and identifying specifically toxic statements. Government and regulatory agencies, including organizations involved in digital security and public space monitoring, information threat analysis centers, and departments developing requirements for the transparency and explainability of algorithms, also need to study information toxicity. This research may also be of interest to the commercial sector, specifically banks and financial institutions that handle customer inquiries, HR services, and companies analyzing employee communications, as well as support and quality control departments. This article substantiates the need to apply methods for interpreting text analysis models based on Transformer architectures. It is demonstrated that modern sentiment models are highly accurate, but remain "black boxes," limiting their use in automated communication content moderation. This article reviews common explainability methods, including attention matrix visualization, local interpretation methods (LIME), and share-based feature estimation (SHAP). The results of an experimental study using a Russian-language sentiment analysis model are presented, demonstrating which tokens have the greatest impact on the final classification. The advantages and limitations of various interpretation methods are described, as well as their applicability to practical user-generated content analysis tasks. The obtained results allow for increased transparency in model decision making, simplified detection of classification errors, and increased user confidence in automated text analysis systems.

title: Stockout-Aware Segmentation and Model Routing for Daily Store-SKU Demand Forecasting
authors: Dmitry O. Lebedev, Anton A. Romanov
abstract: Context: Daily store-SKU demand forecasting for replenishment is challenging due to intermittent demand, promotion effects, and stockout censoring, where recorded sales are a censored proxy for latent demand. Method: We implement stockout-aware training and evaluation using an availability mask so that out-of-stock days are excluded from loss, intermittency statistics, and accuracy metrics. We compare six deployable models (Ridge regression, SARIMAX, single LightGBM, LightGBM hurdle, and two LightGBM ensembles with memory-and window-diversity), propose an interpretable segmentation, and fixed segment-to-model routing policy chosen on validation folds under a runtime constraint. Protocol: Using proprietary daily data (2023-2025; 20 stores, 500 SKUs), we run leakage-safe rolling-origin backtesting over 2025 with a 28-day horizon (13 folds), selecting routing on folds 1-6 and testing on folds 7-13. Results: The window-based LightGBM ensemble attains the lowest aggregated WAPE (0.459), while routing yields competitive aggregated WAPE (0.465), the lowest median MASE (0.614), and reduced runtime relative to the most accurate single model. Implications: The framework supports auditable model selection for heterogeneous retail series while maintaining stockout-consistent evaluation and explicit accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.

title: Digitalized System for Detecting Out-of-Gauge Conditions of Rolling Stock in Railway Transport
authors: Erkin I. Khidirov, Obidjon O. Muhiddinov
abstract: In this article, a digital device is developed based on modern elements for monitoring, determining and controlling the gauge of the rolling stock of the signaling, centralization and blocking system at railway sections. The method of determining the installation ordinate of the rolling stock gauge control device has been improved, using infrared and electromagnetic induction sensors to determine the rolling stock's outsize, comprehensive control and detection of outsize, as well as complete control and control of the process with the help of the created software have been achieved. The created device and its connection to electrical centralization systems have been developed.

title: Digital Model Integration of an Industrial Robot Based on Machine Vision
authors: Daria Fedchenko, Dmitry Sychev, Aleksandra Kulmukhametova
abstract: Integrated digital commissioning enables testing without interfering with the physical manufacturing process within the Industry 4.0 concept. Unity provides real-time visualization and debugging, the prototyping of new algorithms is carried out in a safe virtual environment. Unity a physics-based simulation of the robot and its environment, ensuring the realism of the simulation. System state visualization in Unity enables rapid monitoring and debugging of algorithms. The proposed approach reduces the risk of equipment damage and accelerates development. Algorithms are tested virtually before being deployed on the physical robot. This work presents the development and validation of a digital twin of a robotic sorting complex. A three-dimensional model of a six-axis manipulator was created in Unity, including simulated conveyor lines and virtual sensors. Bidirectional data exchange between the virtual model and Matlab was implemented using TCP/IP. The machinevision algorithm is based on a convolutional neural network that processes the image from the eye-in-hand camera mounted at the gripper and classifies three types of parts against predefined reference patterns. The kinematic model of the manipulator was developed in Matlab using real link parameters, enabling the computation of smooth trajectories for pick-and-drop operations. The simulated digital experiment confirmed high accuracy and reliability of the system. The robot consistently recognized and transported parts to the corresponding conveyors. Operational experience showed that the system withstands prolonged processing cycles without any degradation in recognition quality. Thus, the described digital twin demonstrates the potential to accelerate the deployment of robotic sorting complexes in industrial production.

title: Convolutional Neural Network for Recognizing 3D Models of Reference Objects
authors: Mikhail Yu. Mikheev, Tatyana V. Zhashkova, Sonya Helal
abstract: The widespread adoption of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) equipped with multispectral thermal imagers requires effective approaches for training operators to navigate using nonstandard visual information. Multispectral infrared imagery differs substantially from visible-spectrum scenes and creates cognitive barriers that slow decision-making and increase object-identification errors. This paper proposes integrating a convolutional neural network into a training workflow to automatically recognize and classify synthesized 3D models of reference objects in a multispectral infrared range. A vehicle and a building were selected as the most common landmarks for tactical navigation tasks. A specialized convolutional neural network architecture was developed and optimized for real-time processing of synthetic multispectral infrared images. A methodology for generating a training dataset from synthesized 3D scenes is presented, including comprehensive data augmentation. Experiments demonstrate an overall recognition accuracy of 95.6 percent, outperforming the closest baseline (ResNet-50) by 1.8 percent, while achieving a per-frame inference time of 14.2 milliseconds. A pedagogical experiment with 20 UAV operators showed a 42.7 percent reduction in training time and a 7.8 percent improvement in operator recognition accuracy when using the system with the integrated neural module.

title: Noise2Map: End-to-End Diffusion Model for Semantic Segmentation and Change Detection
authors: Ali Shibli, Andrea Nascetti, Yifang Ban
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Semantic segmentation and change detection are two fundamental challenges in remote sensing, requiring models to capture either spatial semantics or temporal differences from satellite imagery. Existing deep learning models often struggle with temporal inconsistencies or in capturing fine-grained spatial structures, require extensive pretraining, and offer limited interpretability— especially in real-world remote sensing scenarios. Recent advances in diffusion models show that Gaussian noise can be systematically leveraged to learn expressive data representations through denoising. Motivated by this, we investigate whether the noise process in diffusion models can be effectively utilized for discriminative tasks. We propose Noise2Map, a unified diffusion-based framework that repurposes the denoising process for fast, end-to-end discriminative learning. Unlike prior work that uses diffusion only for generation or feature extraction, Noise2Map directly predicts semantic or change maps using task-specific noise schedules and timestep conditioning, avoiding the costly sampling procedures of traditional diffusion models. The model is pretrained via self-supervised denoising and fine-tuned with supervision, enabling both interpretability and robustness. Our architecture supports both tasks (SS and CD) through a shared backbone and task-specific noise schedulers. Extensive evaluations on the SpaceNet7, WHU, and xView2 buildings damaged by wildfires datasets demonstrate that Noise2Map ranks on average 1st among seven models on semantic segmentation and 1st on change detection by a cross-dataset rank metric (average F1 primary, IoU tie-break), while being 13× faster and 3× smaller than the generative diffusion baseline (DDPM-CD) due to its single-step discriminative inference. Ablation studies highlight the robustness of our model against different training noise schedulers and timestep control in the diffusion process, as well as the ability of the model to perform multi-task learning.

title: Bidirectional Trust for Serverless Deployment and Execution in 6G Cloud-Edge Environment
authors: Sanjeet Raj Pandey, Yassine Lazreg, Axel Küpper
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: The increasing adoption of serverless computing has transformed cloud application deployment by enabling scalable, event-driven execution without explicit infrastructure management. However, the opacity of multi-tenant cloud environments introduces significant trust challenges among developers, serverless frameworks, and cloud providers. This paper presents a novel, trust-enabled serverless deployment pipeline that integrates Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) and W3C Verifiable Credentials (VCs) to authenticate and verify all participating parties. In the proposed approach, developers, the serverless framework, and cloud executors exchange VCs to ensure mutual trust during code deployment and execution, thereby strengthening vertical trust across the pipeline. This mechanism enables cryptographically verifiable, tamper-resistant validation of identities and actions, mitigating risks of unauthorized code execution, credential misuse, and infrastructure compromise. The resulting architecture enhances accountability, transparency, and security assurance in serverless ecosystems, establishing a foundation for trusted, decentralized function orchestration in 6 G and beyond, with minimal additional deployment time.

title: Computing the Complex Discrete Spectrum of an Operator
authors: Nadezhda M. Gordeeva, Platon A. Gvozdev, Stepan A. Shchurenkov
abstract: The behavior of plasma in an external electromagnetic field is described by a system of integrodifferential equations for the particle distribution function and the components of the electromagnetic field. Applying the Fourier transform leads to a spectral formulation, which requires finding the zeros of piecewise analytic functions. These dispersion functions are a combination of entire functions and a Cauchy-type integral. The zeros of such functions play a crucial role in solving these problems, as they constitute the discrete spectrum of the corresponding operators. The objectives of this work include: defining the conditions imposed on the functions that allow for the construction of analytical formulas for finding zeros, and providing a detailed description of the algorithm, starting with the computation of the function’s index and determining the number of zeros. To achieve these objectives, an analytical approach is applied, based on new Gakhov-Muskhelishvili-type formulas, along with the theory of analytic functions and computational methods. The paper demonstrates the conditions under which computing the zeros of the considered transcendental functions reduces to finding the zeros of a special polynomial. It also specifies the conditions allowing for the construction of explicit formulas for finding these zeros. This method can be adapted for more complex functions. As an illustration, the developed algorithm, with necessary modifications, is applied to find the zeros of an even function and a function having a finite number of poles. The graphs presented in the article demonstrate the dependence of the found zeros on certain problem parameters.

title: Description of the Behavior of Fault-Tolerant Distributed Computer Systems
authors: Alexandra A. Chudinova
abstract: This research aims to design and evaluate an analytical system for monitoring and predicting the behavior of fault-tolerant distributed computing systems, using the Kubernetes architecture as a case study. Starting from the cluster’s functional schema, which highlights the interaction between control plane, worker nodes, and security components, we propose the introduction of an integrated controller capable of continuously observing the system’s state. The main contribution consists of the development of stochastic models with uncertain functional dependencies, aimed at describing and predicting system behavior under conditions of variability, dynamic load, and partial failures, supporting a predictive approach to reliability management.

title: Neural Networks as Measurement Interpreters and Fuzzy Logic as a Decision-making Mechanism in the Non-destructive Testing of Shaped Cast Blanks
authors: Sergey Kuzovov, Andrey Smykov, Vladimir Zaitsev
abstract: The issues of a combined approach to the construction of intelligent systems for non-destructive testing of shaped cast blanks based on the functional separation of roles between neural network models and a fuzzy inference system are considered. It is shown that the use of "pure" neural network solutions in industrial NDT is fundamentally limited by their statistical nature, dependence on the distribution of training data, and inability to take into account the regulatory, technological, and operational context of decision-making. It is proved that it is advisable to use neural network models as measuring and interpretive converters that translate heterogeneous data from X-ray, ultrasound, thermographic and optical monitoring into the space of interpreted defect parameters and channel reliability indicators. The final decision on the degree of risk of a defect is proposed to be formed on the basis of fuzzy logic, which makes it possible to formalize engineering heuristics, regulatory requirements and the asymmetric cost of errors. The architecture of the system is described in detail, including the stages of fuzzification, rule activation, implication, aggregation and defuzzification according to the Mamdani scheme, and the choice of t-norms and s-norms is justified in terms of physical interpretability and industrial safety requirements. It is shown that the proposed approach provides stability to measurement uncertainty, explainability of conclusions and the ability to adapt to changes in standards without retraining neural network models, which makes it methodologically sound and practically applicable for certified non-destructive testing systems.

title: The RS Publication Roundup
authors: Joanna F. DeFranco
abstract: In this edition of the RS Roundup, we chose to highlight publications deemed popular as the IEEE database. These are articles worth highlighting due to their current popularity and visibility within the research community.

title: A Deep Learning-Based DOA Measurement Method for Underwater Wideband Sources Using Linear Array with Uncertainty Quantification
authors: Jian Fu, Bo Zhang, He Li, Qi Hu, Renhua Peng, Kaiyang Hou, Fan Bai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Direction-of-arrival (DOA) measurement for underwater wideband signals using a linear array is a critical task in oceanic instrumentation, with accuracy directly impacting applications like target tracking and navigation. However, current measurement methods often exhibit significant performance degradation in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) scenes. To address these measurement challenges, this paper proposes a novel deep fusion measurement framework that enhances measurement accuracy and comprehensively quantifies its measurement uncertainty, which introduces a dedicated input feature construction strategy to better preserve measurement information of the wideband signals, along with a physics-informed loss term designed to ensure physical consistency of the measurement model. The framework employs a hybrid deep learning architecture that captures intrinsic angle-amplitude relationships and longrange dependencies across frequency bands through a Cross- Modal Channel Attention (CMCA) mechanism and a Frequency Mamba (Fmamba) module. Functioning as a regression-based measurement system, the proposed approach can estimate an arbitrary number of targets without pre-defining the source count, thereby overcoming the grid precision limitation of classification-based methods. Additionally, uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo dropout is integrated to evaluate how parametric uncertainty affects the model’s predictions. Extensive simulation and lake trial results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art measurement performance in terms of Average Precision (AP), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), while the uncertainty analysis also confirms its value as a robust and self-aware solution for underwater acoustic measurement.

authors: Renaud Bougueng Tchemeube, Philippe Pasquier
