To this end, an in-situ laser-assisted micro/nano scratch tester was developed here, featuring a normal force control uncertainty of 1.6%, and the control logic based on optical-mechanical coupling was planned. The in-situ laser heating strategy maximized the upper temperature limit of the scratch region. Considering the power attenuation of the laser as it passed through the indenter, the temperature field of in-situ laser heating on Zr-based metallic glass (MG) was modeled. And a multi-point temperature synchronous acquisition method based on the thermocouple array was proposed, which accurately measured the spatial and temporal distribution characteristics of the temperature field and realized the calibration of the temperature field model with an uncertainty of 0.6%. Utilizing the developed tester, the scratch-induced deformation behaviors of Zr-based MG at high temperature were investigated. As the laser power increased from 0 W to 10.5 W, the temperature of Zr-based MG entered the supercooled liquid phase region. The average depth of residual scratch grooves increased from 0.45 μm to 5.56 μm, the average width expanded from 4.92 μm to 72.11 μm, and the average COF rose from 0.194 to 0.412. Correspondingly, the dominant scratch-induced deformation mechanism evolved from ploughing to cutting. This study provides an instrument to investigate the microscale deformation behaviors and removal mechanisms of materials in laser-assisted machining.

title: Good Design in an Automated Age Exerts Control Over AI
authors: Ovetta Sampson
abstract: None

title: Hybrid Stream Processing for Runtime Protection in Remote and Infrastructure-Supported Driving
authors: Tarek Stolz, Aliza Katharina Reif, Oscar H. Ramírez-Agudelo, Michael Karl
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: Remote operation and infrastructure-supported driving expand vehicular perception and control through distributed communication between vehicles, infrastructure, and control centres. However, this also creates the need for a cybersecurity management framework that can satisfy the requirements of the cloud and edge environment, as well as rapidly emerging adversarial AI attacks. Since communications between participants in both scenarios can be modulated as data streams, a hybrid data stream-based cybersecurity management framework extending the GALOIS architecture that satisfies requirements is proposed. So far, no concept has been proposed to model a cybersecurity management framework based on a distributed data stream processing architecture that takes into account the interoperability of the different devices in particular. Our results show that real-time analysis of adversarial attacks is feasible but needs a redesign. Our introduced online detection operator outperformed the classical offline detection by 584.3% in latency time.

title: EvaFL: an Efficient Verifiable Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning against Malicious Servers
authors: Xiaoyi Yang, Xing Zou, Xuan Zhao, Qian Chen, Baodong Qin, Yanqi Zhao, Yong Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) preserves client data privacy by distributing model training but remains vulnerable to inference attacks (e.g., gradient inversion). Existing secure aggregation schemes mitigate basic privacy threats, but most of them are under the semi-honest server assumption. Malicious servers can corrupt the global model through forging aggregation results. Moreover, the high interaction rounds and communication complexity of the existing schemes still constrain their feasibility in large-scale distributed deployment scenarios. To tackle these challenges, we propose EvaFL, an efficient verifiable privacy-preserving federated learning against malicious servers, which reduces the communication overhead and privacy threats from malicious severs. We propose the system model of EvaFL and give the concrete protocol. We leverage the linear homomorphism property of Shamir secret sharing under discrete logarithm assumption to reuse the mask seed shares, which avoids the communication overhead caused by share distribution in multiple rounds of iterations. In addition, by integrating consistency checking into the unmasking step, we further reduce one round interaction. To resist malicious servers, we adopt linear homomorphic hash to realize the correctness verification of the aggregation results. Finally, we implement and evaluate our EvaFL based on MNIST and CIFAR10 datasets to show its feasibility for privacy training. The single round aggregation completion time of EvaFL is reduced by 69% compared to BBGLR (CCS 2020) and by 11% compared to Flamingo (S&P 2023).

title: Outshining the Origin: A Pseudo Sources Fusion Approach via Knowledge Distillation with Feature Decoupling for Domain Generalization in Fault Diagnosis
authors: Hairui Fang, Haoze Li, Shupeng Li, Qingyu Li, Yiwen Cui, Fir Dunkin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Deep learning has achieved remarkable success in fault diagnosis but remains vulnerable to distribution shifts due to its reliance on the assumption of independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data. In real-world industrial scenarios, changing operating conditions often result in severe performance degradation. Although domain generalization (DG) offers a promising direction for cross-condition fault diagnosis, existing methods underutilize the diverse knowledge embedded in source models and data, limiting their generalization capability. To address this, we propose KD2G (Knowledge Distillation with feature decoupling for Domain Generalization), a novel approach that constructs pseudo multi-source information via multi-teacher distillation and enhances representation diversity through feature decoupling. KD2G integrates feature engineering into the distillation process by extracting domain-invariant and class-specific knowledge from multiple teachers, enabling the student model to generalize across unseen domains more effectively. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets validate the superiority of KD2G, yielding an average accuracy improvement of 8.5% over state-of-the-art DG methods. This work offers a new perspective on advancing DG theory and provides a scalable solution for robust fault diagnosis under complex operating conditions.

title: Unimodal Model for Emotion Detection in Immersive Virtual Learning Environments Using Spatial Analysis of Hands and Head
authors: Jorge E. Velázquez-Cano, Gabriel González-Serna, Leonor Rivera-Rivera, Nimrod González-Franco, Máximo López-Sánchez, José A. Reyes-Ortiz
abstract: This study introduces a unimodal model for emotion detection in Virtual Reality (VR) environments that depends only on the spatial information from the user's head and hands during interactions within an Immersive Virtual Learning Environment (IVLE). The goal is to eliminate the need for additional sensors, offering an emotional recognition method that can be scaled for multiuser settings. Data on rotation and position from VR devices were collected along with self-reported valence and arousal ratings from 65 participants. Primary and spatial features were extracted, generating mean and median vectors. Random forest regression techniques were then used to predict valence and arousal values in SMOTE augmented data. A paired random pre-augmentation data was used to further test the models in a closer-to-final-implementation scenario. The models achieved accuracies of 70% and 76% for valence prediction using the mean and median vectors, respectively. For arousal, the accuracies were 83% (mean vector) and 87% (median vector). The findings suggest that the median-based approach improves performance, although it involves higher feature dimensionality. This model enables the non-invasive inference of a user's emotional state in VR environments, without cables or extra sensors. This advancement enhances user experience and lowers implementation costs. These results provide a foundation for integrating affective tutors in IVLEs, with potential applications in education and training involving large groups.

title: A Deep Reinforcement Learning-Based Grid Management Framework for PV-Rich Smart Distribution Systems
authors: Souvik Bera, Naran M. Pindoriya
abstract: This article presents a multi-stage deep reinforcement learning framework for volt-var or volt-watt optimization (VVO/VWO) in solar photovoltaic-rich active distribution networks (ADNs) to enhance voltage regulation and minimize network losses. The framework introduces a centralized–decentralized training and execution structure for a multi-agent environment that segregates the objectives—voltage regulation and loss minimization into decentralized and centralized tasks, respectively. This architecture leverages independent learning to reduce training iterations and the computation overheads, particularly in large-scale agent deployments, while sharing limited information during centralized training. A novel contribution factor method based on reward backstepping is proposed to address the non-stationarity caused by independent learning, which enables accurate credit assignment and stable learning. The proposed framework has been evaluated and validated on a practical ADN, a modified IEEE 33 bus, and an IEEE 119 bus system that are segmented into different areas of control (AoC), where each consumer within the AoC comprises local loads and an individual PV-fed inverter, which serves as the primary controlling device for executing VVO/VWO. The results indicate effective reduction of voltage violations and network losses, improved training convergence, and decreased computational requirements compared to existing frameworks.

title: Dynamic Cross Characterization Network for Few-Shot IC Package Substrates Surface Defect Segmentation
authors: Haoyuan Li, Ruiyun Yu, Bingyang Guo
abstract: Due to the widespread use of integrated circuits (IC) package substrates, especially ceramic package substrates (CPS), the industry has raised stringent quality evaluation standards. However, defective packaging substrate samples are scarce and there are many types of defects, so it is difficult for existing semantic segmentation methods to obtain accurate and generalized results on the CPS images. In order to solve the above problems, this article proposes an effective few-shot segmentation method, named dynamic cross characterization network (DCCNet), which can segment untrained CPS defect species using only a small number of labeled CPS samples. First, we introduce a cross sets attention mechanism to enhance the interconnection within the category and better distinguish the differences between the categories. Then, the introduction of the DC block dynamically represents the features, enhances the sensitivity of the features, and reduces the disturbance of differences between classes. Finally, we propose a TB block to deal with feature loss and interclass obfuscation during dimensional change. In addition, we propose a new CPS few-shot segmentation dataset CPSAD-FS to evaluate the proposed DCCNet. Through a large number of comparative experiments and ablation experiments, we have clearly evaluated the state-of-the-art performance of our DCCNet on the CPSAD-FS dataset and verified the effectiveness of each block.

title: From Representation to Clusters: A Contrastive Learning Approach for Attributed Hypergraph Clustering
authors: Li Ni, Shuaikang Zeng, Lin Mu, Longlong Lin
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026
abstract: None

title: An Experience in Research and Analysis of Bioinspired Routing Algorithms in VANET Networks
authors: L. Yu. Studenikin, E. B. Zamyatina
abstract: Wireless computer networks (ad-hoc networks) are currently widely used. Their applications include, for example, the Internet of Things, unmanned vehicle communications, and unmanned aerial vehicle communications. Wireless networks are known to be characterized by node mobility and limited energy reserves. Traditional routing algorithms often fail to ensure reliable and efficient communication. This paper examines the feasibility of using bioinspired algorithms for data exchange in ad-hoc networks, specifically VANETs. The wolf pack algorithm and the whale algorithm are studied using simulation modeling methods. The NETLOGO simulation system was used to conduct simulation experiments. The paper examines how algorithms respond to changes in node density, to changes of travel speed, the detection of emerging obstacles, etc. The experimental results allow us to identify the advantages and disadvantages of each algorithm.

title: Justification of the Combined Neural Network Architecture for Automating Defective Areas Detection Using High-Resolution RGB Images
authors: A. F. Rogachev, K. E. Tokarev, A. Yu. Naumov
abstract: The article discusses the problems of automated identification of various classes of landscape sections based on their color images. Solving the problem of multi-ple classification requires substantiating the architecture and hyperparameters of deep neural networks, their parameterization and deployment on mobile devices, taking into account the features of the analyzed objects. The purpose of the study is to substantiate the neural network architecture for mobile cyberphysical systems for automated detection of color image areas. The quality and performance of the projected neural network significantly depend on the characteristics of the train-ing dataset, therefore, various methods of preprocessing the source images are used, including image dimensionality correction. Well-known neural network architectures, including fully connected, convolutional, and recurrent layers and their combinations, were analyzed. An analysis and justification of the basic ar-chitecture of the neural network were carried out to identify the characteristic are-as of color images. To eliminate the influence of stochasticity of the initial choice of its weights, a deterministic approach is justified and mathematical dependen-cies are proposed for choosing the initial values of the weights of a neural net-work. The neural network quality values were obtained: average accuracy in the training sample: 0.958; maximum accuracy in the training sample: 0.967; average accuracy in the test sample: 0.912; maximum accuracy in the test sample: 0.921.

title: Box-Free Model Watermarks Are Prone to Black-Box Removal Attacks
authors: Haonan An, Guang Hua, Zhiping Lin, Yuguang Fang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Box-free model watermarking is an emerging technique to safeguard the intellectual property of deep learning models, particularly those for low-level image processing tasks. Existing works have verified and improved its effectiveness in several aspects. However, in this paper, we systematically investigate the vulnerability and demonstrate that box-free model watermarking is prone to removal attacks, even under the real-world threat model such that the protected model and the watermark extractor are in black boxes. Under this setting, we carry out three studies. 1) We develop an extractor-gradient-guided (EGG) remover and show its effectiveness when the extractor uses ReLU activation only. 2) More generally, for an unknown extractor, we leverage adversarial attacks and design the EGG remover based on the estimated gradients. 3) Under the most stringent condition that the extractor is inaccessible, we design a transferable remover based on a set of private proxy models. In all cases, the proposed removers can successfully remove embedded watermarks while preserving the quality of the processed images, and we also demonstrate that the EGG remover can even replace the watermarks. Extensive experimental results verify the effectiveness and generalizability of the proposed attacks, revealing the vulnerabilities of the existing box-free methods and calling for further research.

title: Deconvolutive optimized general linear Chirplet transform and its application in hydrocarbon detection
authors: Huixing Zhang, Wangyang Wang, Bingshou He, Yancang Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Time-frequency analysis (TFA) methods are widely used techniques for local frequency analysis and seismic attribute extraction, playing a significant role in hydrocarbon-bearing reservoir prediction and geological structure interpretation. The general linear Chirplet transform (GLCT) has been widely adopted in time-frequency analysis due to its superior time resolution. However, the GLCT suffers from the limitation of fixed time-window width, which restricts its adaptability to signals with varying frequency components. Furthermore, GLCT can only maintain optimal time-frequency resolution within localized frequency bands and is prone to introducing artifacts in low-frequency regions. To further enhance the time-frequency resolution, we propose a new TFA method named deconvolutive optimized general linear Chirplet transform (DOGLCT). The methodology consists of two main steps: first, a four-parameter-controlled Gaussian window function is introduced as the time window for optimized GLCT (OGLCT). The width of this four-parameter Gaussian window adaptively adjusts in response to frequency variations, thus ensuring guaranteed time-frequency resolution across arbitrary frequency bands. Subsequently, the time-frequency spectrum (TFS) obtained from OGLCT undergoes deconvolution with the point spread function (PSF), ultimately yielding the refined TFS of DOGLCT. The TFA results of synthetic seismic signals demonstrate that DOGLCT outperforms the conventional GLCT in terms of time-frequency resolution and effectively addresses the issue of low-frequency artifacts. Tests on noisy seismic data validate the superior noise robustness of DOGLCT. Finally, the application of DOGLCT to field data for hydrocarbon detection confirms the enhanced time resolution of the proposed method, enabling precise identification and delineation of hydrocarbon-bearing reservoir distributions.

title: Search Deterrence by Time-Limited Retail Channels: Information Design and Discount Strategy
authors: Weiqian Lu, Lindong Liu, Xiaoshuai Fan
abstract: This paper investigates how time-limited retail channels influence consumer search behavior and sellers' operational decisions. We model a live-streaming channel as an exploding channel (EC), a time-limited channel that simultaneously delivers product information and price discounts, and incorporate it into a classical consumer search framework with a normal channel (NC). Using a game-theoretic consumer search model, we identify a novel search deterrence mechanism whereby the EC's information design and discount structure can partially or fully suppress consumers' incentive to search in the NC. When NC search costs are high, the EC completely eliminates search in the NC. As EC search costs increase, sellers transition from offering no discounts to introducing price discounts, while information provision becomes less essential. When NC search costs are low, optimal seller strategy requires jointly deploying information design and price discounts, producing a counterintuitive form of price discrimination in which high-valuation consumers purchase at lower prices than low-valuation consumers. At the system level, the impact of EC adoption is non-monotonic. The EC can improve consumer surplus under pure information design, yet price discounts may constitute nominal reductions that leave consumer surplus unchanged. When channel search costs converge, total welfare may deteriorate, and competing firms' simultaneous EC adoption can trigger a prisoner's dilemma.

title: Minimizing the Age of Incorrect Information for Unknown Markovian Source
authors: Saad Kriouile, Mohamad Assaad
abstract: The problem of minimizing the Age of Information has been extensively studied in real-time monitoring applications. In this paper, we consider a scheduling problem in which a central monitor decides, at each time slot, whether to schedule a Markovian source to receive new status updates, with the goal of minimizing the Mean Age of Incorrect Information (MAoII). When the source parameters are known, we show that the optimal solution is a threshold-based policy and propose a low-complexity algorithm to determine this optimal threshold. When the source parameters are unknown, the main challenge lies in balancing exploration and exploitation. Specifically, the scheduler must estimate the unknown parameters (exploration) while simultaneously minimizing the MAoII (exploitation). In our model, the monitor can only explore a source by scheduling it. Using a naive greedy approach risks prematurely stopping exploration if, at a given time, the estimated parameters suggest that the optimal action is never to schedule the source. To address this, we develop a novel learning algorithm that overcomes this issue and prove that its regret, compared to a genie-aided solution, grows logarithmically with the time horizon. Finally, we present numerical results to demonstrate the performance of our proposed policy against baseline approaches.

title: Terminal Force Estimation of Hydraulic Manipulators via Fusion of Offline Calibration and Online Adaptation for Multi Source Uncertainty
authors: Bolin Sun, Xiansheng Wang, Min Cheng, Fu Zhang, Bing Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Terminal force estimation is critical for control and monitoring during the operation of hydraulic manipulators (HMs). Existing methods often struggle to maintain accuracy under coupled multi-source uncertainties (sensor noise and modeling errors) and sudden external forces. To address this challenge, an estimation method based on the Kalman filter (KF) is proposed, which utilizes sequential calibration and state-dependent fusion of noise covariance matrices (NCMs). First, a sequential NCM calibration procedure is designed based on dynamic parameter identification, where steady and dynamic calibration trajectories are used to mitigate coupling among major uncertainty sources and obtain more accurate offline-calibrated NCMs. Second, a state-dependent NCM fusion strategy is developed, which adjusts the weights between offline-calibrated and online-adaptive NCMs according to system states (joint/hydraulic cylinder velocity and the estimated terminal force rate) within a KF framework. Experiments on an HM test rig validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, showing that, compared with a KF using fixed NCMs and an adaptive KF, it reduces the mean force estimation error under dynamic trajectories by up to 41.1%. In sudden force scenarios, the maximum estimation error is reduced by up to 58.7% (from 332.5 N to 137.4 N).

title: Evaluating Neutral Grounding Resistor Sizing in Medium-Voltage Systems: Is 400 A Always the Correct Answer?
authors: Cory A. Helfrich, Rakan El-Mahayni, Ahmed Alhumoud
abstract: IT IS COMMON INDUSTRY PRACTICE TO HAVE 5-KV AND 15-kV systems low-resistance grounded. These low-resistance–grounded systems are designed to limit ground fault current to a range between 100 A and 1,000 A, with 400 A being typical. One of the main purposes of using low-resistance grounding is to limit ground fault currents to hundreds of amperes instead of thousands of amperes. The damage to the equipment connected to the system, particularly rotating machines, is limited by reducing the ground fault current. As 20 kV, 1,000 A resistors become more available; low-resistance grounding is also being used on 34.5-kV systems, both to reduce ground fault energy and to reduce concerns regarding step- and touch-potentials at 34.5-kV equipment. Since the damage caused by ground fault currents increases with the magnitude of the current, it is desirable to limit the ground fault current to a value as small as possible without compromising the ability of protective devices to detect the fault and without exposing the system to dangerous overvoltages. This article presents a procedure to determine the optimum size of the neutral-grounding resistor (NGR) in medium-voltage systems commonly found in industrial plants and electrical-distribution systems.

title: Management Model in Remote Application Servicing Systems with Conditional Priority
authors: Vakhid Zakirov
abstract: The widespread use of information and communication technologies in service systems has led to the emergence of remote service. A distinctive feature of this service is the multi-stage nature of request processing. The increase in the number of types of remote request processing leads to a sharp increase in the number of users of these systems. This, in turn, causes request overload in the systems and reduces system efficiency. Due to the urgency of the problem, numerous studies have been conducted to improve system performance. These methods rely on coding, maintenance, load balancing, and increasing the number of service devices. Unlike existing methods, we propose a request management system for congestion based on priority request servicing. This management system enables full service of conditionally priority requests when all serving devices become busy. A conditional priority badge is assigned to users successfully served by the first stage system. To determine the effectiveness of the proposed request management method, simulation modelling was used due to the cumbersome and complex nature of solving the systems of linear equations. These equations are based on stationary Markov birth-death processes. Assigning a "conditional priority" to successfully processed requests at the initial stage of multi-stage processing and providing preferential service to such requests in the event of overload will increase system efficiency by 5-7%. An algorithm for implementing remote processing of requests with conditional priority is proposed.

title: Toward Carbon-Aware UAV Security: Lightweight ML for GPS Spoofing Detection
authors: Dimitrios-Evangelos Gkogkos, Panagiotis Papadimitriou, Sophia Petridou
publish: 2026 29th Conference on Innovation in Clouds, Internet and Networks (ICIN)
abstract: Detection of Global Positioning System (GPS) spoofing is critical for UAV safety and autonomy under tight resource and energy budgets. We present a lightweight, carbon-aware machine learning (ML) pipeline using receiver-level Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) features (e.g., latitude, longitude, altitude). Leakage-aware evaluation restricts scaling, RFECV, and tuning to stratified <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K$</tex>-fold CV on the train-validation split; the test set is used once. We benchmark Logistic Regression (LR), Ridge Classifier, Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD), and linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) as linear baselines, alongside Gaussian Naive Bayes (NB) as a lightweight probabilistic alternative, measuring accuracy, latency, energy, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C O_{2}$</tex> with CodeCarbon. On a UAV dataset with 19 features, several lightweight models achieve near-perfect accuracy under this leakage-aware scale-RFECV-tuning workflow, showing receiver-level signals are highly informative for spoofing detection. We further define a lightweight operating-point <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k_{\mathrm{L}}$</tex> as the smallest feature subset within 0.5 percentage points of the RFECV optimum <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k^{*}$</tex>, enabling real-time deployment with fewer features, lower complexity, and reduced emissions.

title: Hybrid Active Sampling Framework Using Gaussian Process Regression for Efficient Prediction of RCS
authors: Min Zhao, Zi He, Da-zhi Ding, Dan Guo, Jia Zhai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: A hybrid active sampling framework (HASF) combined with Gaussian process regression (GPR) is presented to achieve crucial sampling and efficient prediction of radar cross section (RCS). Specifically, the proposed framework involves three sequential stages to realize the global exploration, the local refine search, and the supplemental sampling. In Stage-Ⅰ, a novel fast leave-one-out (FLOO) method significantly enhances the computational efficiency of conventional LOO. The distance-based acquisition function is designed as the selection criterion. The establishment of a relative error threshold facilitates the realization of adaptive key sampling for diverse curves, thereby avoiding redundancy. In Stage-Ⅱ, the amount of local minimum neighborhoods that need to be finely searched can be determined adaptively according to data characteristics. The acquisition function based on distance and variance is constructed. It is employed as the selection criterion in order to execute a refine search in the selected local minimum neighborhoods. The same strategy as Stage-Ⅰ is employed in Stage-Ⅲ. Compared to conventional predict methods, HASF achieves higher prediction accuracy by the comprehensive sampling of both global and local regions. In comparison with other active sampling methodologies, HASF-FLOO has superior computational efficiency. The proposed framework has also demonstrated its effectiveness on complex models and measured datasets.

title: Limitations of MAMPs and Holland-Simpson Thin-Wire Models and a New Unified Wire Formalism in FDTD
authors: Duncan Lo, Michal Okoniewski
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Thin-wire subcell models enable the representation of wires with radii smaller than the grid resolution in the finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) method. While numerous formulations have been proposed, the differences between the numerical behavior of these models remain poorly understood. This work investigates two widely used models—the Modified Assigned Material Parameters (MAMPs) and Holland–Simpson formulations—by benchmarking their TMz plane wave scattering responses for wires of varying radii against analytical solutions. Numerical experiments show that MAMPs produces non-physical scattering for wires of small radius (i.e. r/Δx ≪ 1). The cause of artificial scattering is explained by showing that MAMPs erroneously enforces perfect conductors at locations where parameters are modified. Alternatively, Holland’s formalism requires a decreased time step compared to the standard Courant-Friedrichs-Levy (CFL) condition to maintain stability for large radius (i.e. r/Δx > 0.1). Even with this reduced time step, the model fails to produce accurate results. This failure is attributed to the Holland model’s limitation in representing higher-order scattering modes using a single stored current. To address these limitations, a novel thin-wire formalism is proposed for accurately modeling wires across all thickness regimes.

title: Far-field characterization of high-gain antennas at 300 GHz band based on a photonics-based near-field measurement
authors: Yusuke Tanaka, Kento Ishihara, Kota Miyake, Keizo Inagaki, Shintaro Hisatake
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper presents a photonics-based near-field measurement approach for characterizing far-field radiation patterns of high-gain antennas operating at 300 GHz. Utilizing an electro-optic (EO) probe for electric near-field sensing, the system enables stable and precise acquisition of amplitude and phase over long measurement durations, which is essential for evaluating antennas with nominal gains exceeding 40 dBi. Far-field patterns reconstructed from near-field data using cylindrical mode expansion showed excellent agreement with direct far-field measurements conducted at an outdoor site with an antenna separation of 53 m. This result confirms that tabletop photonics-based near-field systems can serve as practical alternatives to outdoor far-field ranges at sub-terahertz frequencies. Quantitative evaluation using the equivalent noise level (ENL) metric demonstrated high pattern fidelity, with average ENL values below −35 dB within the −10 dB beamwidth region. Fine structural features, including low-level sidelobes, were also accurately reproduced. Comparisons with the ITU-R F.699-8 reference radiation pattern revealed narrower predicted beamwidths and underestimated sidelobe levels, likely due to model limitations and radome effects, suggesting the need for updates above 86 GHz. The proposed method offers a scalable and practical solution for antenna metrology in frequency regimes where conventional direct far-field measurements and CATR systems face significant implementation challenges.

title: CADNM: A Chain Active Distribution Network With Soft Open Points for Optimal Power Schedule of Expressway Considering Multi-Uncertainties
authors: Shuaibing Li, Jiuding Tan, Yi Cui, Zhe Qiu, Yuchen Zhu, Yongqiang Kang, Haiying Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: To address the challenges of limited resilience and high operational costs in current expressway power supply systems under extreme conditions, a chain active distributed network model featuring dynamic SOPs is introduced. This model leverages flexible interactions among network nodes to facilitate optimal power flow distribution and energy dispatch. An uncertain dataset, based on the Wasserstein distance, is constructed to account for the diverse characteristics of power sources and loads on expressways. Using this dataset, a “Traffic-Electrical” coupling model for typical power scenarios is developed to identify the optimal SOP installation locations under improved flow capturing location model constraints, thus forming an active network with SOPs. A two-stage optimization model targeting both the “robustness” and “economy” of the power supply system is designed with the support of a novel “NSGA-II-PSO” heuristic algorithm. Simulations of the “Taohuaping-Dingyuan” segment of the G30 “Lian-Huo Expressway” demonstrate the model’s effectiveness, with a 9.74% reduction in average voltage offset and a 3.78% decrease in dispatch costs compared to traditional networks, confirming its robustness, flexibility, and cost-effectiveness.

title: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Subspace Clustering of Subspaces: Unifying Canonical Correlation Analysis and Subspace Clustering
authors: Paris A. Karakasis, Nicholas D. Sidiropoulos
abstract: We introduce a novel framework for clustering a collection of tall matrices based on their column spaces, a problem we term Subspace Clustering of Subspaces (SCoS). Unlike traditional subspace clustering methods that assume vectorized data, our formulation directly models each data sample as a matrix and clusters them according to their underlying subspaces. We establish conceptual links to Subspace Clustering and Generalized Canonical Correlation Analysis (GCCA), and clarify key differences that arise in this more general setting. Our approach is based on a Block Term Decomposition (BTD) of a third-order tensor constructed from the input matrices, enabling joint estimation of cluster memberships and partially shared subspaces. We provide the first identifiability results for this formulation and propose scalable optimization algorithms tailored to large datasets. Experiments on real-world hyperspectral imaging datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior clustering accuracy and robustness, especially under high noise and interference, compared to existing subspace clustering techniques. These results highlight the potential of the proposed framework in challenging high-dimensional applications where structure exists beyond individual data vectors.

title: Janus Behavior of Stochastic and Deterministic Operation in a MOSFET
authors: Sang-Won Lee, Ho-Young Maeng, Seung-Il Kim, Yang-Kyu Choi
abstract: Whether a deterministically operating MOSFET can also exhibit stochasticity remains an open question. During conventional MOSFET operation, carrier transport is governed by drift and diffusion, leading to largely deterministic switching behavior. This work demonstrates that the operation of a MOSFET with a single transistor latch (STL) can become either more stochastic or more deterministic. This depends on how structural design and electrical bias conditions modulate the pre-latch band-to-band tunneling (BTBT) level. In particular, junction engineering and gate-bias modulation control the pre-latch BTBT level. Enhanced pre-latch BTBT primes the floating body (FB), and increases the seed current (Iseed) to trigger impact ionization (II), thereby narrows the statistical distribution of latch-up for more deterministic STL operation. In contrast, when pre-latch BTBT is suppressed, the distribution broadens and the operation becomes more stochastic. This Janus-type controllability significantly expands the application space of MOSFETs into previously unexplored domains.

title: Flexible Capacitive Pressure Sensors with Microstructured-Porous Dielectric Layers for Pressure, Shear, and Proximity Detection_supp1-3685355.docx
authors: Maria B. A. Rosa
abstract: Flexible capacitive pressure sensors (FCPS) are in the spotlight due to their wide range of applications. However, today’s solutions still suffer from limited linearity, restricted multifunctionality, and complex fabrication processes. To address these challenges, this work proposes the development of flexible capacitive pressure sensors with a tunable microstructured dielectric layer fabricated using sugar-templating, isopropanol-induced porosity, and soft lithography. The devices exhibit wide linear range, achieving high sensitivity (up to 3.695 kPa<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> in the low-pressure regime and 0.014 kPa<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> in the high-pressure regime), low hysteresis (around 3%), fast response and relaxation times (90 / 69 ms), and a low limit of detection (3 Pa). This geometric modification, combined with their full integration in a custom platform, results in sensors capable of detecting normal pressure, shear forces, and proximity distances up to 27 mm. The proposed approach makes the sensors suitable for applications in wearable electronics, health monitoring, soft robotics, and human-machine interfaces, where linearity, multifunctionality, and customization are essential.

title: Cost-Efficient Design Closure of Antennas Using Multi-Fidelity EM Analysis and Deep-Learning-Based Space Mapping
authors: Slawomir Koziel, Anna Pietrenko-Dabrowska
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Numerical optimization is integral to modern antenna design. Practical design closure is typically achieved by fine-tuning initial layouts obtained through geometry evolution and parametric studies guided by engineering insight. This stage is commonly performed using gradient-based optimization, which entails substantial computational cost due to repeated electromagnetic (EM) simulations. This letter proposes a new approach to accelerated EM-driven parameter tuning that combines multi-fidelity EM analysis with deep-learning-based space mapping (SM). Our method employs reusable deep neural network (DNN) surrogates to implement multi-point correction of a low-fidelity EM model via non-parametric SM, which replaces the original high-fidelity antenna model within the gradient-based optimization loop. High-fidelity EM evaluations are performed only intermittently to update the DNN surrogate, with particular emphasis on improving accuracy near the current iterate. The retraining process is guided by distance-based weighting of the available training samples. Our framework is extensively verified using three antenna devices, including multi-band and broadband structures. The results demonstrate the outstanding efficiency of the approach, with average optimization costs equivalent to only 15 high-fidelity EM simulations and substantial acceleration over conventional high-fidelity-only optimization, achieving average speedups of up to 87%. Moreover, multiple optimization runs targeting different design specifications confirm the reusability of the trained DNN surrogate without incurring additional computational expense.

title: Six-DoF Coupled Dynamics Modeling and Intelligent Vibration Suppression of CMG With the Flexible Vibration Isolator
authors: Ming Lu, Jiankun Yang, Ke Wang, Chaoxu Mu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The control moment gyroscope (CMG) is widely used for high-precision attitude control in aerospace applications due to its excellent torque output and energy efficiency. However, the micro-vibrations generated during CMG operation can propagate through flexible vibration isolation platforms, which may affect the performance of sensitive spacecraft payloads. To address this critical issue, this article proposes a systematic algorithm that combines coupled dynamic modeling with intelligent control. First, a six-degree-of-freedom (six-DoF) dynamic model accounting for the flexibility of the vibration isolator is developed, which combines the flywheel rotor, gimbal system, and the vibration isolator with a flexible platform to characterize their coupled dynamics. Based on this model, an active-passive isolation control algorithm is designed, which combines feedforward disturbance compensation with a policy iteration algorithm based on adaptive dynamic programming (ADP). The feedforward component enhanced by neural networks effectively approximates and cancels out the interference caused by CMG in real time, while ADP optimization ensures adaptive and optimal vibration suppression. The simulation results demonstrate the performance of the proposed algorithm, confirming a significant reduction in vibration transmission and a marked improvement in isolation efficiency.

title: AffNet: Designing Multi-headed Affinity and Adaptive Thresholding for Unsupervised Homophily Estimation
authors: Indranil Ojha, Kushal Bose, Swagatam Das
abstract: The Message-passing (MP) framework contributes to the profound success of the Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). MP predominantly operates on homophilic graphs where the adjacent nodes are highly likely to have identical class labels. However, for heterophilic graphs containing adjacent nodes frequently with different class labels, numerous sophisticated algorithms have been proposed. A prior idea about the homophily of a graph helps choose the algorithm to be used. The estimation of homophily relies on the node labels which, practically, are often unavailable. Hence, we remain clueless about which GNNs should be applied to the input dataset. To mitigate the issue, we propose a novel and efficient method to measure the homophily without having prior information on the node labels. Our method employs trainable affinity vectors equipped with nodes apart from the pre-existing node features. The affinity vector of a node represents the feature vector that the node expects in a suitable neighbor. We measure affinity scores between nodes as the similarity between the affinity of one node to the features of another node. In this connection, we define a unique homophily measure based on the affinity scores of nodes with themselves. Extending the concept, the multi-headed affinity vectors are designed to represent a diverse set of choices the nodes have to connect to other neighbors. Furthermore, we design an adaptive strategy to apply learned thresholds by going beyond the prevailing ones that use fixed thresholds. The estimated affinity scores are subsequently considered as the probability scores determining the existence of edges. In this context, we propose a robust architecture AffNet that can be used to simultaneously estimate homophily in an unsupervised setting, solve the link prediction task and build recommendation systems. Applying our framework to several standard and large-scale datasets, we obtain impressive performances compared to various state-of-the-art methods.

title: Multi-Frequency Magnetic Induction-based Miner Identification and Underground Positioning System
authors: Mudra Chavda, Khan Mohammad Ehshan, Sandeep Rathour, Mohammad Zeeshan, Lalit Kumar Baghel, Shahid Malik
abstract: Precise monitoring of the location of miners in underground mines is essential for ensuring safety, particularly during emergencies. Low-frequency magnetic induction (MI) provides a reliable and robust solution for ranging and positioning in underground environments due to robustness against dust, humidity, and line-of-sight (LoS) obstructions. Usually, a high resonant frequency is desirable for miniaturized MI systems to achieve long-range positioning; notably, it increases the complexity of the electronic system. Moreover, the present MI-based system provides the positioning, not the identity, of the miner. This paper introduces a multi-frequency miner identification and MI-based wearable 2D positioning system designed for underground mining environments. It introduces a novel approach, utilizing uniaxial portable mobile transmitter coils attached to helmets of miners, alongside biaxial stationary receiving coils placed at fixed locations within the mine. Additionally, a modulation-based miner identification system assigns a unique modulating frequency to each miner, whereas the resonance frequency serves as the carrier frequency. Moreover, this technique combines received signal strength (RSS) from orthogonally placed biaxial receiver coils to determine the position of the miner with an accuracy of 97.63%, maintaining an average Euclidean error of 4.48 cm in the test area of 4.6×4.6 m2 with the developed algorithm. It also achieves a resolution of 10 cm in radial distance and 15◦ in angular orientation with respect to the observation point.

title: GRIN Polarization-Independent Collimating Metalens for Directivity Enhancement of a 0.3-THz RTD
authors: Takehito Suzuki, Yuki Saito, Kazuisao Tsuruda, Kento Sato
abstract: Manipulating terahertz waves is crucial for the practical use of continuous-wave (CW) terahertz sources in industrial applications. We present a gradient-index (GRIN), polarization-independent collimating metalens composed of 91 double-sided circular copper meta-atoms patterned on a cycloolefin polymer film. The GRIN design provides refractive indices increasing from the periphery toward the center, with the central meta-atoms exhibiting a refractive index of 6.2, a reflectance of 1.1%, and a transmittance of 94.3% at 0.312 THz. These optical properties are achieved by simultaneously controlling the relative permittivity and permeability to similarly high values. Measurements verify that integrating the fabricated GRIN collimating metalens with a resonant tunneling diode oscillating at 0.312 THz enhances directivity by a factor of 3.1. The proposed approach enables compact, high-directivity terahertz optical components suitable for integration with various CW sources in 6G and beyond wireless communication systems.

title: CoMAEA: A Collision-Avoiding Multi-Agent Evolutionary Algorithm for Coverage Path Planning_supp3-3665409.mp4
authors: Ting Huang
abstract: Efficient and safe path planning for multiple autonomous agents, such as unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), is essential in many real-world applications. The objective is to coordinate these agents to cover a target area with minimal cost. However, most existing multi-agent coverage path planning (MACPP) methods rely on purely spatial planning and neglect spatio-temporal interactions among agents, which often result in unsafe paths and a high risk of inter-agent collisions during execution. To address this challenge, we propose a new problem formulation, termed collision-aware MACPP (CaMACPP), which incorporates both spatial coverage efficiency and collision avoidance. To solve this problem, we introduce an efficient multi-agent evolutionary algorithm to minimize costs while avoiding collisions. The algorithm first introduces a trapezoidal decomposition strategy to partition complex environments into convex subareas. Then, a cooperative evolutionary algorithm is designed to optimize the inter-area visiting orders for multiple agents and the intra-area coverage modes assigned to each agent. Next, a spatio-temporal collision avoidance mechanism is incorporated to detect and resolve conflicts between agent paths. The proposed algorithm is extensively evaluated against the state-of-the-art MACPP methods to demonstrate its effectiveness in generating collision-free paths with minimal total cost. The results show that it achieves zero collisions in all tested scenarios, reduces the average path length by 15.9%, and decreases coverage overlap by 58.1%.

title: Cell-Free Integrated Sensing and Communication: Principles, Advances, and Future Directions
authors: Diluka Galappaththige, Mohammadali Mohammadi, Gayan Amarasuriya Aruma Baduge, Chintha Tellambura
abstract: Cell-free integrated sensing and communication (CF–ISAC) combines CF architecture with ISAC. CF employs distributed access points (APs), eliminates cell boundaries, and enhances coverage, spectral efficiency (SE), and reliability. ISAC unifies radar sensing and communication, enabling simultaneous data transmission and environmental sensing within shared spectral and hardware resources. CF–ISAC leverages these strengths to improve spectral and energy efficiency (EE) while enhancing sensing in wireless networks. As a promising candidate for next-generation wireless systems, CF–ISAC supports robust multiuser communication, distributed multistatic sensing, and seamless resource optimization. However, a comprehensive survey on CF–ISAC has been lacking. This article fills that gap by first revisiting CF and ISAC principles, covering cooperative transmission, radar cross section (RCS), target parameter estimation, ISAC integration levels, sensing metrics, and applications. It then explores CF–ISAC systems, emphasizing their unique features and the benefits of multistatic sensing. State-of-the-art developments are categorized into performance analysis, resource allocation, security, and user/target-centric designs, offering a thorough literature review and case studies. Finally, this article identifies key challenges such as synchronization, multitarget detection, interference management, and fronthaul capacity and latency. Emerging trends, including next-generation antenna technologies, network-assisted systems, near-field CF–ISAC, integration with other technologies, and machine learning (ML) approaches, are highlighted to outline the future trajectory of CF–ISAC research.

title: Metrology-Driven Comparison of Biomechanical Model of Fingers and Thumb: Evaluating Marker Configurations for Kinematic Measurements
authors: Saqib Zafar, Juri Taborri, Stefano Rossi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate recording of hand joint kinematics is crucial for fields such as clinical diagnostics, ergonomics, and sign language recognition. It relies on the precise placement of markers and the number of markers used to capture joint movements effectively. This study presents a simplified marker-based biomechanical model of the hand designed to compute 22 joint angles using only 24 passive retroreflective markers, following a one-marker-per-segment approach. Kinematic data was collected from ten participants performing predefined physiological movements. The proposed biomechanical model was compared with three established biomechanical models employing one, two, and three-markers-per-segment on the fingers. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this study is the first to directly compare these marker configurations simultaneously under identical experimental conditions. Range of motion (ROM) and joint variability were analyzed using repeated-measures ANOVA, while temporal trajectory differences were assessed via one-dimensional Statistical Parametric Mapping (SPM). Comparative analysis revealed no significant differences in ROM, variability, and joint angle trajectories across models, offering a robust and efficient approach for hand motion analysis suitable for functional, ergonomic, and AI-driven applications.

title: 12/10 Switched Reluctance Motor Capable of Operating as Induction Motor
authors: Akira Kohara, Noboru Niguchi
abstract: In this paper, a switched reluctance motor capable of operating as an Induction motor is proposed. This motor has a rotor that combines the salient poles of a switched reluctance motor with the squirrel-cage structure of an induction motor, enabling its operating characteristics to be varied by changing the applied current pattern. The structure and operational principle of this motor are described. Finally, the N-T characteristics are computed by finite element analysis under two types of control patterns.

title: Seamless Switching Control Utilizing Zero-Voltage Vector Intervals for PMSM Winding Reconfiguration
authors: Xinshuai Zhang, Qingbo Guo, William Cai, Yongxi Yang, Minghao Zhou, Lei Yang, Yuchuan Lin, Liangji Ma, Tongfei Sheng
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Stator winding reconfiguration technology has attracted extensive research owing to its capability to dynamically regulate the torque-speed output characteristics of motors and expand their high-efficiency operation range. This paper proposes a fast and seamless switching control strategy for the series-parallel winding reconfiguration system of permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs), where fully controlled power electronic devices are employed as reconfiguration switches. The key innovation lies in achieving winding reconfiguration entirely within the zero-voltage vector (ZVV) interval of a single SVPWM period. Compared with conventional winding switching methods, this approach yields three salient advantages: first, it eliminates potential surge voltages without resorting to additional hardware snubber circuits; second, it circumvents the issues of asymmetric operation and torque interruption inherent to conventional schemes; third, it reduces the reconfiguration transient duration from the millisecond level to several microseconds. In this study, the minimum ZVV interval required is only 8 μs, within which the intermediate transition state for reconfiguration occupies merely 5 μs. The detailed mechanism and implementation are described, while simulation and experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Arctic Sea Ice Thickness Retrieval From SMOS Using Unsupervised-Classification–Based Selection of Representative Tie Points
authors: Jiaxing Li, Shengkai Zhang, Feng Xiao, Tong Geng, Jie Ma, Fei Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Retrieval of Arctic thin sea ice thickness (SIT) from Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity (SMOS) L-band brightness temperatures is highly sensitive to tie-point selection, whereas conventional fixed tie-point methods cannot adequately capture the strong regional and seasonal variability of Arctic brightness temperature signatures. This study proposes a regionalized multi-tie-point (MTP) retrieval framework for Arctic thin SIT during the freezing seasons from 2010 to 2023. A pan-Arctic MTP dataset is first constructed from SMOS brightness temperature observations and sea ice concentration time series. Representative tie points are then identified using three unsupervised clustering strategies: K-medoids, K-means + K-medoids, and K-means + affinity propagation (AP). These representative tie-points are further incorporated into a weighted retrieval scheme to estimate SIT. The results show that the clustering-based strategies effectively preserve the dominant spatial heterogeneity of tie-point characteristics and reduce retrieval uncertainty. Among them, the K-means+AP approach provides the best balance between retrieval accuracy and computational efficiency, while preserving regional heterogeneity more effectively. Validation against Operation IceBridge measurements shows that the proposed method improves retrieval accuracy by approximately 20% compared with the University of Bremen product, and by 11.7% compared with the University of Hamburg product for collocated thin-ice samples. The proposed framework provides a more accurate and stable approach for large-scale Arctic thin SIT retrieval from SMOS observations.

title: Noncooperative Spacecraft Pose Estimation under Low Sampling Frequency: A Variational Integrator-Aided Factor Graph Optimization Approach
authors: Hang Chen, Jiafeng Xu, Yueyang Liu, Xiaodong Shao, Qinglei Hu, Dongyu Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Visual navigation is the prerequisite for proximity operations. However, for defunct spacecraft removal and repurposing, insufficient onboard computational resources and high visual processing overhead lead to low-frequency measurements, thereby reducing navigation accuracy and reliability. This paper proposes a factor graph optimization framework incorporating a dual quaternion variational integrator (DQVI) for relative pose estimation. This framework leverages the kinetic energy conservation characteristics of a tumbling spacecraft, maintaining high estimation accuracy with minimal computational cost even at low sampling rates. Specifically, a pose increment constraint equation between adjacent timestamps using dual quaternion variational integration is constructed, based on which a binary constraint factor is further established. To fuse visual data, two pseudo-measurement factors are designed for both cameras and LiDAR. Pose increments, along with the complete pose (including relative attitude and position) are then embedded into optimization variables. A full-state factor graph model is developed, allowing the visual navigation to be handled. Finally, semi-physical experimental validations demonstrate a 63.58% attitude accuracy improvement and a 64.48% enhancement in position estimation under 4Hz sampling rates. It is shown that the proposed method enhances reliability under low sampling frequency conditions.

title: Co-Design of Parallel Applications on Heterogeneous Computing Systems Using Reconfigurable Algorithmic Skeletons in OpenCL
authors: Carlos Alfonso Acosta-León, Rina Surs
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Reconfigurable heterogeneous computing systems (RHCS) combine FPGAs with programmable devices such as CPUs and GPUs to exploit parallelism through coordinated processing. However, the complexity of parallel programming and FPGA design makes unified hardware/software development difficult. This work presents aSkelHCoRe, a framework of Reconfigurable Algorithmic Skeletons for Co-design in Heterogeneous Systems, which leverages Cole's skeleton paradigm to provide high-level abstractions for structured and transparent parallelism. Implemented in OpenCL/Python, it offers reusable Pipeline and Master-Slave skeletons that enable the creation of high-performance applications independent of specific hardware architectures. Experimental evaluations using multiple hardware/software configurations demonstrate reduced execution times when hardware kernels are combined with software-based I/O. The results confirm that aSkelHCoRe simplifies heterogeneous programming, shortens development effort, and allows seamless migration of functionalities between software and hardware during design-space exploration.
